text: **Records of Rebirth (唐 唐临)** Compiled by Tang Lin, Minister of Personnel **Volume 1 of Records of Rebirth** All beings that have breath and life possess consciousness. With consciousness comes action, and one receives retribution according to the good or evil of their actions. This is like a farmer sowing seeds, for whatever is sown is reaped. This is the constant principle of all things, and there is nothing to doubt. The highest wisdom understands its fundamental source, knowing it without seeing it. The lowest ignorance is unaware of its traces, wandering blindly and never returning. These are matters beyond words. People of middle capacity cannot fully comprehend. They encounter phenomena by chance, and from these encounters, doubts arise. These doubts take many forms, each person holding their own differing views. The Buddhist scriptures discuss their distinctions, totaling sixty-two views, from which heretical beliefs arise. I, the author, am among those of middle capacity, and fortunately, I have awakened to a small part of it. I have seen many who do not believe in cause and effect, and though their views are numerous, they all assert that good and evil actions have no retribution. The doctrine of no retribution has three main forms: 1. Naturalism, which holds that there is no cause and effect, and one should simply follow their desires and wait for events to unfold. 2. Annihilationism, which claims that upon death, the body and consciousness cease to exist, leaving no one to experience suffering or joy. Since there is no experiencer, there is no cause and effect. 3. The doctrine of no retribution, which denies any consequences for actions.

Observing that some people today cultivate morality, if they are poor and lowly, they die early; or they commit evil deeds, if they are wealthy and noble, they live long. Because of this incident, one knows that there is no cause and effect. I secretly think that Confucian texts discuss the rewards and punishments for good and evil deeds, which are many. Those who are near in time receive their reward immediately; those who are in the middle receive their reward after many years; those who are far away receive their reward in the descendants. Those who receive their reward immediately, such as the ruler of Chu who swallowed a leech and all chronic diseases were cured, the ruler of Song who did not pray and the omen stars retreated, the person with a bad tooth who was punished immediately upon turning around, Zhao Gao who caused chaos and soon his family was destroyed, and so on. Those who receive their reward after many years, such as Wei Ke who married his concubine and finally saved his army, Sun Shu who buried a snake and eventually enjoyed many blessings, Emperor Yuan of Han who poisoned Prince Li and caused disaster, Qi who killed Peng Sheng and was punished by having a pig appear, and so on. Those who receive their reward in the descendants, such as Fu Fu who was respectful to three commands and spread Confucius's teachings, Deng Xun who saved a thousand people each year and left a legacy of peace and prosperity, Chen Ping who had secret plans and knew he would have no descendants, Luan Yan who was extravagant and was punished with disaster, and so on. As for Yu Shun, who ascended to the throne through filial piety, and King Wen of Zhou, who received his mandate through benevolence and wisdom.

Jie and Zhou perished their countries through cruelty. You and Li perished through debauchery and indulgence. The virtues and achievements of the Three Dynasties ensured their long-lasting rule. The tyrannical Qin emperor, though powerful, saw his dynasty fall within a generation. Such cases as these are numerous. Even though the scale may differ, they are all verifications of good and evil. But when it comes to the principles of kingship, it is related to the will of heaven. In ordinary discourse, it is not appropriate to say. What is recorded now merely takes these subtle verifications, in order to inspire those of the same kind and warn and instruct descendants, as evidence in the world of humans and spirits. The teachings of the Buddha are nothing but cause and effect. Cause is the action, and is the retribution. Nothing is not a cause, and nothing has a cause that does not have a retribution. However, the teachings of retribution also have three kinds: immediate retribution, where good and evil deeds done in this life are experienced in this life; rebirth retribution, where deeds done in this life are experienced in a future life, depending on the nature of the deeds; and future retribution, where good and evil deeds done in past lives are experienced in multiple lives.

Therefore, in the present actions, one cannot immediately receive retribution. Sometimes the next generation may suffer, or after five or ten lifetimes, the consequences may finally manifest. All these are called delayed retribution. In these three kinds of retribution—immediate, intermediate, and delayed—they encompass all phenomena, leaving nothing out. Thus, the various views are now greatly enlightened. However, today's common people are still confused by this concept. They focus on causes and forget the results, doubting what they hear but believing what they see. Therefore, when they hear about delayed retribution, they either accept it or reject it. But when they see evidence, they are amazed, sigh, and believe. In the past, there were notable figures such as the Jin dynasty scholar Xie Fu, the Song dynasty chancellor Fu Gao, the Southern Dynasties literary figure Bao Yan, and the Southern Qi scholar Lu Guo. Some were respected figures of their time, while others were famous scholars of the era. They all compiled collections of miracles, such as the *Guanshiyin Yingyan Ji* (Records of the Miracles of Avalokiteshvara), the *Xuan Yan Ji* (Records of Miracles) by King Jingling Xiao Ziliang of the Qi dynasty, and the *Mingxiang Ji* (Records of Supernatural Events) by Wang Yan. All these works are meant to demonstrate good and evil, to encourage and warn future generations, truly making those who hear deeply reflect. I have been inspired by their spirit and intent and also wish to encourage others. I have recorded what I have heard and compiled this record. I have also described what I have experienced and the reasons for what I have heard and seen. The words are not embellished, and the facts are presented clearly and accurately. May future readers pay attention to it.

In the capital, there was a highly respected monk named Xinxing. Originally, he was a monk at the Fa Zang Temple in Xiangzhou. At first, his mother had no children, and she was worried for a long time. A monk passed by and advised her to recite the name of Guanyin Bodhisattva. The mother said she would pray at night. Soon after, she became pregnant and gave birth to Xinxing. From a young age, he was intelligent and extensively studied Buddhist scriptures and treatises. His understanding exceeded that of others. He believed that the Buddha's teachings aimed at saving and guiding beings, sometimes according to the nature of the listener, he pointed out the path, and sometimes according to the times and circumstances, he decided on the appropriate dharma. Now, it has been a long time since the Buddha's time, and the natures and times have changed greatly. If lower beings practice higher methods, the method may not suit their nature, and they might go astray. Therefore, he compiled and collated scriptures and treatises to verify what should be learned by people, compiling thirty-six volumes titled 'Ren Ji Lu' (Human Collection Record). At the beginning of the Kaihuang era, the Left Chancellor and Duke of Qi heard of his great reputation, reported to Emperor Wen, and summoned him to the capital. He stayed at the True Silence Temple built by the official. Xinxing then compiled four volumes of the Three Stages Method based on the Vinaya and Sutras. The main idea is to encourage universal reverence, recognize the root of evil, contemplate Buddha-nature, prescribe medicine according to the illness, and teach the immediate one vehicle. From all over the country, those who are brave and diligent in practice all followed him. Xinxing often practiced asceticism, begging for food, and worshipped six times a day. He worked hard to stabilize his mind, focusing on emptiness and true wisdom. Whenever he sat in meditation and preached, he often saw four green-clad boys holding flowers standing by. Once, while sitting in meditation with his disciples in the hall, everyone suddenly smelled a strange fragrance, and light filled the hall. They were all amazed and asked Xinxing about it.

Xin Xing commanded his disciple Monk Yong to inquire about something. Yong said, "I saw a transformed Buddha coming down from the sky, arriving before the Master, touching his head, and giving him a prophecy. It was as if the Buddha also touched my head and gave me a prophecy. The rest of the scene was the same as what I described." Later, Yong managed his group of disciples and retreated to Mount Taibai. One day, he called together the monks and said, "I will return to the capital with you." The monks, respecting Yong, all agreed. They then descended the mountain and stayed overnight in Wugong. Before dawn, they set out, and Yong told the group, "Strive, everyone. Tonight we must enter the city." They reached a place called Cao, and Yong said, "The city gates are closed." They then stayed at an inn. In the evening, Yong wept and said, "It is too late." When asked why, he did not respond. The next morning, they entered the city and arrived at Zhenji Temple. At dusk the previous night, Xin Xing had passed away. The monks at the temple were surprised to see Yong and asked why he had come. Yong replied, "I saw many people carrying incense, flowers, banners, and canopies coming from the west and entering Kaiyuan Gate, heading towards Zhenji Temple. I suspected the Master wanted to leave, so I came. Last night, I saw the Master leading his retinue westward and looking back to say goodbye to me. That's why I know it's too late." Initially, the monks in the capital who doubted Xin Xing's teachings discussed it and referred to the *Fayuan Zangjing* (Transmission of the Dharma Treasury), which states that if a person has heard the true Dharma in a past life, their ears will be perfectly aligned. They then examined Xin Xing's skull and found that his ears were indeed perfectly aligned. They all felt ashamed and regretful, finally believing and respecting him. Initially, Xin Xing's followers lived in five temples in the capital. Later, the number of temples increased, but they were still known as the Five Masters. (As told by Old Monk and Uncle Lin.)

The Monk Hui Ru of the True Silence Temple in the Capital City In his youth, Hui Ru was diligent in his ascetic practices and studied under the teacher Xin Xing. After Xin Xing's death, Hui Ru continued to follow his teachings. During the reign of Emperor Yang of Sui, he engaged in meditation and cultivation of concentration. For seven days, he remained motionless, which amazed the crowd who thought he had entered a meditative state. Later, Hui Ru opened his eyes and cried, with tears flowing freely. The monks were surprised and asked him why. He replied that his feet were burning and in pain, and he would speak after examining the wound. The monks asked further, and Hui Ru said that he had been invited by King Yama. He had walked the path for seven days, and King Yama asked if he wished to see any deceased friends. Hui Ru answered that he wanted to see two people. King Yama immediately sent for one, but only a turtle appeared, licking Hui Ru's feet before leaving with tears in its eyes. Another person was said to have committed heavy sins and could not be called. Hui Ru was ordered to go and see them. A messenger led Hui Ru to the prison gate, which was very solid. The messenger called the guard, and someone answered. The messenger told Hui Ru to quickly avoid the path and not block the gate. As soon as Hui Ru moved aside, the gate opened, and a great fire flowed out, like a star forging iron, striking Hui Ru's feet. He brushed the blanket over it, raised his eyes to look at the gate, which was already closed. He could not see anyone. King Yama gave him thirty bolts of silk, but Hui Ru refused, saying it had already been sent to the back room. The monks rushed there, and when they looked, the silk was on the bed. Hui Ru's burned feet were as large as coins, and it took more than a hundred days to heal. He died at the beginning of the reign of Emperor Gaozu of Tang. The True Silence Temple is now known as the Transformation and Measure Temple. (This temple was established by Duke Qi, my maternal grandfather, and I often visited it. I often heard my uncle speak of this.)

A Great Monk from the State of Jiang. The monk named Seng Che was young and skilled. He built a hall on the western slope of Lonely Mountain, surrounded by many trees. The location was quite suitable for mountain living. Once, while Seng Che was walking through the mountains, he saw a leper in a cave. The leper had ulcers and a foul odor and begged Seng Che for food. Moved by compassion, Seng Che called him out and took him back to his temple. He built a cave next to the temple for the leper, provided him with clothing and food, and taught him to recite the Lotus Sutra. This person could not read or write and was also dull-witted. Seng Che taught him word by word, which required a great deal of effort. However, he never tired. After reciting about half of the sutra, the person began to have dreams where someone taught him. From then on, he gradually became more intelligent. By the time he had recited five or six chapters, his wounds began to heal. By the time he finished reciting the entire sutra, his beard and eyebrows had grown back, and he had regained his normal weight and appearance. He was also able to heal others. Once, when Seng Che himself was suffering from swelling, he sent this person to recite incantations, which proved effective.

The monk said so himself. Later, Fang Renyu became the governor of Qin Prefecture. He submitted a memorial to establish a monastery founded by Monk Chè, which was named Sunken Spring Temple. Initially, this place had no water. Monk Chè used to carry water from far away to supply his own needs. One day, suddenly there was a sinkhole, and water emerged from it. Hence, the temple was named after the sinkhole. Monk Chè devoted himself to promoting goodness and personally practiced Zen meditation. He was revered by people near and far, like a father. In the first month of the second year of the Yonghui era (651 AD), he suddenly gathered his disciples and said he would die. Then he sat upright on a rope bed, closed his eyes, and remained motionless. The weather was clear and bright, and flowers appeared like snow, fragrant but not dissipating, covering an area of about two li in diameter. The leaves on the trees had a white color, like light powder for three days, then returned to their normal color. Monk Chè had passed away. Until now, three years have passed, and he still sat alone as before, without emitting a foul odor. Only tears flowed from his eyes. (His disciples Shi Qin and others, as well as the people of the state, all said this.)

There was a virtuous nun from Hedong who often recited the Lotus Sutra. She sought out a skilled calligrapher and offered several times the usual payment. She specially prepared a clean room and asked him to write this scripture. She bathed once, burned incense to perfume her clothes, and also had a wall in the writing room broken through to the outside, adding a bamboo tube. She ordered the calligrapher to hold the bamboo tube and exhale outside whenever he wanted to breathe out. She completed one scroll of the scripture in eight years, offering the utmost respect and veneration. The monk Fahuan from Longmen often gathered a large audience to lecture on the Lotus Sutra. Because this nun's copy was meticulously accurate, he sent someone to request it. The nun firmly refused to give it, and Fahuan criticized and reproached her. The nun, unable to refuse, then personally delivered it. When Fahuan opened and read it, he saw only yellow paper with no writing. When he opened other scrolls, they were all the same. Fahuan and his followers felt ashamed and fearful, and immediately returned it to the nun. The nun wept and accepted it, washed the box with fragrant water, bathed, and placed it on her head. She walked around the Buddha, reciting sutras, for seven days and nights without stopping. Afterward, when she opened it, the text was as before. In the second year of the Zhenguan era, Fahuan himself came to tell the story.

A monk from Ren Shou Temple in Pu Prefecture, named Shi Daoxian, was young, intelligent, and fond of learning. He was highly respected by the local community and had delivered the *Nirvana Sutra* more than eighty times, earning a reputation for his mastery. In the second year of the Zhen Guan era, Cui Yizhi served as the magistrate of Yu Qing County. The people invited him to give a lecture on the sutra. At the beginning of the lecture, the monk wept and told the audience, "The teachings of the Buddha are distant in time, and the subtle meanings are difficult to grasp. What is commonly taught by ordinary people is not worthy of being a model. One should rely on faith and devotion to understand the teachings. Today's discourse will end at the point of the lion's roar. After the time has passed, everyone should reflect on it in their hearts." As he reached the part about the lion's roar, he suddenly passed away without any illness. The monks and laypeople were shocked and mournful. Cui Yizhi himself walked barefoot to accompany the monk to his burial place on the southern slope of a mountain. It was November, and the ground was frozen. When the body was laid down, flowers sprang up from the ground, resembling small lotuses. There was a flower on each of the head and limbs. Cui Yizhi found this miraculous and had someone guard it at night. The guard fell asleep, and someone stole the flowers. In the morning, it was seen that flowers had grown all over the body, totaling more than five hundred stems. After seven days, the flowers withered and dried. (Both Cui Yizhi and the monks and laypeople all said so.)

The Monk Dao Ying of Hedong Dao Ying, a monk of Hedong, practiced Chan meditation from a young age, taking the refinement of the mind as the foundation of his practice. He did not concern himself with external decorum, yet he had an exceptional understanding of the deep meanings of Buddhist scriptures and precepts. Monks and nuns from far and near sought his judgment, and he would reply, "You have not yet formed your doubts; it is better to ponder them. Formulate your questions clearly before coming to ask." Those who returned to ask often found that they had resolved their doubts through reflection and left satisfied. Some, however, still could not understand and returned to ask again. Dao Ying would then explain the key points to them, and they all returned delighted and enlightened. Once, Dao Ying traveled on the Yellow River with others. The boat sank in midstream, and everyone else died. Monks and laypeople saw him disappear and cried by the river. At that time, it was late winter, and the river ice was just beginning to melt, with the riverbanks still frozen. Dao Ying walked out of the water onto the shore, walked through the ice, and returned. The people on the riverbank were filled with awe and joy, and they all wanted to cover him with their clothes. Dao Ying said, "My body is still warm; do not cover me with clothes." He slowly emerged and returned, without any signs of cold. His body appeared as if it had been roasted by fire. Those who understood recognized it as the result of deep meditation. At times, Dao Ying worked as a cowherd or driver, ate garlic and rice, and sometimes wore secular clothes, with his hair growing several inches long. Once, he visited Ren Shou Temple, and Dao Xuan respectfully arranged for him to stay. Dao Ying said he would seek food later. Dao Xuan remarked, "A person of great virtue may not show signs of hunger, but how can one avoid criticism?" Dao Ying smiled and replied, "Dao Xuan, your mind is racing with worry, not even pausing for a moment's rest, yet you are hungry. Why do you trouble yourself?" Dao Xuan was deeply impressed. Dao Ying died during the Zhenguan era, and Fazhuan, as well as monks and laypeople, all said this.

A monk named Zhi Yuan from Youzhou, skilled and well-learned, resolved during the Dàyè period of the Sui Dynasty to carve stone scriptures and store them, in preparation for the potential extinction of the Dharma. He then carved out rock in the northern mountains of Youzhou to create a stone chamber, polished the four walls, and wrote the scriptures on them. He also took flat stones, polished and wrote on them separately, and stored them inside the chamber. When each room was full, he sealed the door with a stone and secured it with iron. At that time, the emperor visited Zhuo Commandery, and Xiao Yu, the Secretary of the Board of Works and the younger brother of the empress by the same mother, was deeply devoted to Buddhism. He informed the empress about Zhi Yuan's project, and the empress donated a thousand bolts of silk, along with the remaining money and goods, to assist in its completion. Xiao Yu also donated five hundred bolts of silk. The court and the people heard about this and competed to donate. Thus, Zhi Yuan was able to complete his task. Zhi Yuan once thought that with so many laborers, both monks and laypeople had gathered, and he wanted to build a wooden Buddha hall, dining halls, and sleeping quarters in front of the rock. However, he was concerned that it would be difficult to distinguish between wood and tiles, and that it would divide and waste the scriptures and materials.

Therefore, construction could not begin. One night, a fierce rainstorm with thunder and lightning struck the mountain. The next morning, the sky cleared, and it was seen that there were thousands of large cypress and pine trees at the foot of the mountain, carried away by the water and accumulated along the road. In the area east of the mountain, there are few forested areas, and cypress and pine trees are especially rare. Monks and laypeople were startled, not knowing where they came from. Tracing the traces, it was found that they came from far away in the west, from the western mountain, where collapsed banks and fallen trees had drifted here. People far and near were amazed and admired, saying it was divine assistance. The official then ordered craftsmen to select the trees, and the rest were distributed to the townspeople, who were delighted and together helped build the halls and buildings. Soon after, it was completed, all according to their wishes. The official built seven chambers filled with stones. He died in the thirteenth year of the Zhenguan era. His disciples continued his work (the Chancellor Li Xuanzang of the Palace Department, the Magistrate Cai Xueming and others, all told me this. In the nineteenth year, I followed the imperial carriage to Youzhou, and asked the locals, who also said the same, but due to military affairs, they could not say).

At the end of the Eastern Wei dynasty, people from the capital Ye ventured into the Western Mountains to mine for silver ore. They had not yet fully exited the mine when it collapsed. One person was trapped behind, with the entrance blocked by rocks, preventing escape. However, the person suffered no physical harm. At the site of the collapse, a small hole remained open, allowing a faint light to be seen. The trapped person, realizing there was no logical way out, focused on reciting Buddhist mantras. The person’s father, hearing that his son had been buried, was unable to find the body. Being destitute, he could not perform rituals to benefit the deceased. He carried a simple bowl of food to a monastery, asking one monk to eat with him. Most monks preferred generous offerings and were unwilling to accept the meager meal. The father, holding the bowl of food, wept bitterly. One monk, moved by his plight, accepted the invitation. After eating, the monk chanted prayers for the father. Then, he left. Meanwhile, the son, still alive in the mine, suddenly saw a monk through the small hole. The monk entered the mine, carrying a bowl of food, which he offered to the trapped person. After eating, the person no longer felt hungry and sat in meditation, maintaining focus for over ten years. When Emperor Wen of the Northern Qi ascended the throne, he built a cool hall in the Western Mountains. The skilled workers cleared the collapsed rocks and discovered that the person in the mine was still alive. They rescued the person and brought him home. The parents were overjoyed, and the family’s fortunes improved. (According to Lu Chengye, the Military Governor of Yongzhou, who recounted the story, the person was a scholar who had been sent to the Western Mountains.)

During the Northern Qi period, there was a man from Jizhou who was sent with an army to attack Liang. After the battle was lost, he was captured and enslaved. His parents, living in the countryside, did not know of his fate and believed he had died. To seek blessings for him, they built a brick stupa. Once the stupa was completed, they held a vegetarian feast, attended by hundreds of monks and laypeople. While they were sitting to eat, they heard knocking at the door. The owner’s father went to see and found a monk with a very dignified appearance. The monk said to the owner, "I beg for a vegetarian meal of millet porridge, which you can wrap in a handkerchief. And I also ask for a pair of shoes." The owner invited the monk to stay and eat, but the monk declined, saying, "I wish to leave early; I don’t have time to sit and eat." The owner complied, wrapping the porridge in a new cloth and presenting the shoes to the monk. The monk accepted and left. At the time of the feast, the owner’s son was in the southern marshes, herding cattle for his master. Suddenly, he saw a monk holding a wrapped bundle of porridge and a pair of shoes. The monk approached him and asked, "Do you also wish to return home to see your parents?" The slave wept and said, "I dare not hope for it." The monk gave him the porridge and told him to sit and eat. After finishing, the monk gave him the shoes and had him put them on. Then, the monk spread his monk’s robe on the ground and told the slave to sit on it. The monk took the four corners of the robe, grasped them together, and lifted it up, moving it about two zhang and placing it on the ground. When the slave opened his eyes, he did not see the monk or the robe, but found himself outside his house. Upon entering, he saw that the crowd was still eating. His parents were overjoyed and asked him what had happened. He explained the whole story. They looked inside the handkerchief and found the remaining porridge and the shoes, which were the same ones they had given to the monk. The villagers were shocked and rushed to become devout believers. This day was the sixth day of the month, so they named the stupa the Sixth Day Stupa. The stupa still stands today, and the village still tells of it.

During the Northern Qi period, there was a man from Jizhou who joined a military campaign against the Liang dynasty. He suffered a defeat and was captured, becoming a slave. His parents, remaining in the village, had no news of him and believed him to be dead. In order to seek merit for his soul, they built a brick stupa. When the stupa was completed, they held a vegetarian offering, attended by hundreds of monks and laypeople. As they were just beginning to eat, they heard a knock at the door. The master of the house went to see who it was and found a monk, whose appearance was very elegant. The monk said to the host, "I beg for a vegetarian meal of millet porridge, which you can wrap in a cloth handkerchief, and also ask for one pair of shoes." The host invited him to stay and eat, but the monk refused, saying, "I wish to leave early; I have no time to sit and eat." The host followed his request, wrapping the porridge in a new cloth and presenting it along with one pair of shoes. The monk accepted them and departed. At that time, it was the hour of the vegetarian offering. The master’s son was in the marshes of southern Jiangnan, tending cattle for his master. Suddenly, he saw a monk holding a wrapped bundle of porridge and one pair of new shoes, arriving at the slave’s location. The monk asked, "Do you also wish to return and see your parents?" The slave wept and said, "I dare not even hope for that." The monk gave him the porridge and told him to sit and eat. After finishing, he gave him the shoes and told him to put them on. Then, he spread his monk’s robe on the ground and told the slave to sit upon it. The monk took the four corners of the robe, grasped them all together, and lifted it up, waving it. The robe moved about two zhang before touching the ground. The slave opened his eyes and saw neither the monk nor the robe, but found himself already at the front door of his home. Entering, he saw the gathering still eating. His parents were surprised and questioned him, and he explained the entire story. Looking inside the cloth, they found the remaining porridge and shoes, which were the very ones he had given to the monk. The village was astonished and all became devout believers. This event took place on the sixth day of the month, and so they named the stupa they had built the "Six-Day Stupa." The stupa still stands today, and the story is still passed down in the village.

During his humble beginnings, Emperor Wu of Liang met a poor scholar. After ascending to the throne, while strolling in the imperial gardens, he saw someone pulling a boat and asked about him. The scholar, still poor and lowly, was summoned by an imperial edict, which stated that he could come to pay respects the next day and would be appointed as the magistrate of a county. The scholar went in response to the edict, but due to some reason, he could not meet the emperor. He tried repeatedly but always encountered some affairs that prevented him from seeing the emperor. Frustrated, he wondered and asked the monk Baoyi. Monk Baoyi was just preaching to a large audience, with thousands of listeners. The poor scholar could not get in. Monk Baoyi called out to the crowd, "There is someone who wants to come and ask a question. Please open the path for him." The crowd then opened the way. As the scholar approached, Monk Baoyi met him and said, "Are you here to ask why you didn't get the magistrate's position? You will never get it. You will only receive empty favors." Monk Baoyi explained, "In the past, the emperor was the host of a vegetarian feast, and you promised to donate five hundred coins, but you never gave it. That is why you only received the promise of an official position, and you will never get it." The scholar heard this and finally left. The emperor also stopped pursuing him. The story is still being told by people in the Jiangdong region to this day.

Yan Gong of Yangzhou. Originally from Quanzhou, he was rich in wealth but without brothers. His parents loved him, and he never disobeyed their words. In the early years of the Tain Jian era during the Chen dynasty, Gong was in his early twenties. He asked his parents for permission to get five thousand coins to go to Yangzhou to buy goods. His parents agreed. Gong sailed on a boat carrying money and went down to Yangzhou. After traveling several tens of miles, he encountered a boat carrying turtles in the river, which intended to sell them in the market. Gong asked to know the reason and thought the turtles would die, so he asked to redeem them. The turtle owner said, "My turtles have large heads, and a thousand coins each." Gong asked how many there were, and the owner answered there were fifty. Gong said, "I have exactly five thousand coins. I am willing to redeem them." The turtle owner happily took the money and gave the turtles to Gong, then left. Gong released all the turtles into the river and continued his journey with an empty boat to Yangzhou. The turtle owner, traveling more than ten miles away from Gong, had his boat sink and he died. That day, Gong's parents were at home. At dusk, there were fifty black-clothed guests who came to the door to stay overnight. They also brought five thousand coins and gave them to Gong's father, saying, "Your son is in Yangzhou. These coins are attached for safe return. We wish to receive them according to the amount." Gong's father was surprised and startled, doubting that Gong was dead. He investigated it, and the guest said, "My son is fine. He does not need the money, so it is returned." Gong's father accepted it, remembering it as the original capital, but all were wet. He kept the guests to prepare food, and the guests stopped. The next morning they departed. After more than a month, Gong returned, and his parents were very happy. They then asked about the source of the attached money. Gong answered that there was none. His parents described the appearance of the guests and the date of the attached money, which was the day Gong redeemed the turtles. Thus, they knew the fifty guests were all the redeemed turtles. Father and son were amazed.

Yan Fa-hua, a devout Buddhist, gathered his fellow believers to Yangzhou to build a study hall dedicated to writing the *Lotus Sutra*. They then moved their family to Yangzhou, where their wealth gradually increased. They constructed large, elegant rooms for writing scriptures, which were well-supplied and often attended by dozens of students. The people of Yangzhou, both laypeople and monks, revered him, and he was known as Yan Fa-hua. One day, a relative borrowed 10,000 coins for scriptures. Yan reluctantly gave the money to him. The borrower accepted the money and returned home by boat, but the boat capsized in midstream, causing the borrowed money to fall into the water. Miraculously, the boatman did not drown. It was said that Yan then entered the treasury and saw 10,000 wet coins like those that had come out of the water. Yan was very surprised by this and later realized that the wet coins were the ones he had lent. Another time, a merchant arrived at Gonghu Lake and offered wine and food at a temple, presenting additional offerings. That night, he dreamed that a deity returned the offerings to him, saying, "Please take this for me to offer to Yan Fa-hua for the use of writing scriptures." The items he had offered to the deity were all before him. The merchant was amazed and delivered the items to Yan, who gave even more generously. Later, Yan went to the market to buy paper for writing scriptures. He just happened to have a little money when suddenly, he saw a person holding 3,000 coins and gave them to Yan, saying, "Buy paper." After he spoke, the person disappeared, and the coins were left in front of Yan. Such strange occurrences were not one. At the end of the Kaihuang era of the Sui dynasty, Yan died, but his descendants continued his work. Bandits who came to Jiangdu at the end of the Sui dynasty all agreed not to enter Yan Fa-hua's neighborhood, and the people of the neighborhood relied on him and were saved. His family continues to write scriptures without stopping. Both the prefecture and the county saw this, and people in the capital also knew about it. Prince Xiao Rui, the Duke of Guo, was the most detailed and thorough in his knowledge of this.

Records of Karmic Retribution, Volume 5 During the middle of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, Cui Yanwu, the governor of Weizhou from Boling, was on an inspection tour. He arrived at a town and was startled with joy. He told his attendants, "I once lived in this town and was married. Now that I know where my former home is, I will return there." Turning his horse, he entered a long alley and turned into a house, knocking on the door. The elderly homeowner came out to greet him respectfully. Cui Yanwu entered the house and first ascended to the main hall. He looked up at the eastern wall and saw a raised area about six to seven feet from the ground. He said to the homeowner, "I once hid my copy of the Lotus Sutra and five gold hairpins in this wall. They are hidden in the high spot." He also mentioned that the last part of the seventh scroll of the scripture had been partially burned, causing some words to be lost. He said, "Until now, every time I recite this scripture, I always forget the part at the end of the seventh scroll." He then ordered his attendants to chisel through the wall. They found the scripture case, and when they opened the seventh scroll, they found the gold hairpins, just as he had said. The elderly homeowner wept and said, "My deceased wife, when she was alive, used to read this scripture. The hairpins are also hers. She died during childbirth and vanished. She did not seduce you, but she showed you the place where she hid these items." Cui Yanwu also said, "There is a槐 tree in front of the courtyard. When I was about to give birth, I cut my own hair and placed it in a high place in the tree. I asked someone to check if the hair was still there, and they found it." The homeowner was moved to tears and joy. Cui Yanwu left some clothing and gave the homeowner generous gifts before departing. According to Cui Shangshi, a high-ranking official, this story was told to him. He also mentioned that he had seen Lu Wenli in the past, who had a similar account. However, the name of the Qi Prefect was not known, and the story was still recorded according to Cui's account.

During the Dàyè period of the Sui dynasty, a traveling monk arrived at a temple dedicated to a mountain deity and requested lodging. The temple official said, "There is no other place to stay; only the eaves of the temple can be used." However, recently, those who stayed there always died. The monk said, "Don't worry." The official, having no choice, allowed him to stay and provided a bed under the eaves. That night, the monk sat upright and recited sutras for about one watch. He heard the sound of jade pendants in the room. Soon, the deity appeared and bowed to the monk. The monk said, "I have heard that recently, those who stayed here always died. Did you harm them? Please protect me." The deity said, "When they die, I hear their disciples' voices and become afraid of death. I do not kill them. Please do not worry." The monk then invited the deity to sit and talked to him as if he were a human. After a long time, the monk asked, "I have heard that people say the mountain deity can subdue ghosts. Is this true?" The deity said, "I have little merit and can do so. Do you want to see the deceased?" The monk said, "Two of my fellow monks died earlier. I wish to see them." The deity asked for their names and said, "One has already been reborn in the human world, and the other is in prison. Because of heavy sins, he cannot be seen. You can go and see the one in the human world." The monk was very pleased and went out with the deity. They did not walk far before arriving at a place where they saw many temples and prisons.

Fire was very bright. The deity led the monk into a courtyard. From a distance, he saw a person in the fire, calling out but unable to speak, with a form so changed it was unrecognizable, and the flesh burned and smelled of charred meat, which was heart-wrenching. This was it. The master no longer wanted to observe further. Feeling sorrow and compassion, he sought to leave. Soon he arrived at the temple. He sat down with the deity again and asked if there was a way to save his fellow student. The deity said, "It can be done. If one writes the Lotus Sutra, they will be saved." Soon it was dawn, and the deity bid the monk farewell and entered the hall. In the morning, the temple official saw that the monk was not dead and found it strange. The monk then explained. He then wrote the Lotus Sutra. Once the sutra was completed, it was decorated, and he took the sutra to the temple to stay overnight. That night, the deity appeared as before, happily bowed, and inquired about the monk's purpose. The master then told him the story. The deity said, "I know. The master wrote the sutra, completing the title, and the other has already been saved and has long since been reborn." However, this place is not clean, and it is not suitable for the sutra. I hope you will return and take the sutra to the temple. They talked for a long time until dawn, and then they bade farewell and left. The monk then took the sutra to the temple. (Zhang Deyan, the former governor of Yanzhou, who now serves in Hangzhou, knows all about this. He recounted it himself.)

Xiao Jing, the Grand Master of the Imperial Academy, was from Lanling. He was the grandson of Emperor Wu of Liang and the fifth son of Liang Wang Kui. After the fall of the Liang dynasty, he entered the Sui dynasty. His sister became the empress of Emperor Yang of Sui. He grew up in great wealth and status, and his family revered Buddhism. During the Dali era, he believed that by reciting the Lotus Sutra, he constructed a Multitude of Treasures Pagoda according to the text of the Sutra, made of sandalwood. The pagoda was about three feet tall, and the top was made of wood to resemble the Multitude of Treasures Buddha. After several years, his nephew Quan, who lived in the house, suddenly saw a sandalwood stupa in the grass of the front courtyard upon waking up one morning. Under the canopy was a stone Buddha statue, made differently from those in China. The facial features resembled those of a foreigner, and the eyes were made of silver, with a black, lustrous appearance as if naturally formed. Quan ran to tell Xiao Jing, who was surprised and delighted upon seeing it. He took the canopy back and tried placing it on the pagoda, and it fit perfectly, as if originally made. Although the wood color was slightly different, the pagoda shape was even more exquisite. Placing the Buddha statue inside the pagoda also fit perfectly as if originally made. Xiao Jing was very happy and believed it was due to his sincere devotion. The Buddha statue contained over a hundred relics. Xiao Jing's young nun sister suspected and thought that the foreign monk often said the relics cannot be broken by hammering. She then tried taking thirty of them and struck them with an axe on a stone.

The relics were nowhere to be found. The woman searched on the ground and only obtained three or four pieces, while the rest were lost and could not be found. Fearing this, she informed Jing. Jing went to the pagoda to look and found that the relics were all there as before. From then on, Jing began to recite the Lotus Sutra once, continuing until the end of his life. In the eleventh year of the Zhenguan era, he fell ill. Empress Xiao and his brothers and nephews visited him. Jing met with them, and each was instructed to light incense, then they parted. Only his brother Zonggong Yu and his daughter who became a nun were left behind, and they were instructed to light incense and recite sutras. Soon after, Jing said to his nun, "I want to go; the Bodhisattva Universal Worthy is coming to welcome me. I am in the eastern courtyard. You can welcome him." The nun went as instructed to welcome him, but he did not return. Jing said, "This courtyard is not clean, he will not come. I will go to him. You all live well." Then he parted from Yu and others, rose, knelt long with palms together, facing west. Soon after, he lay down and passed away. He left a will: to be carried alone in a cart, dressed in the clothes he wore, with women not attending the funeral, no meat offerings, and the grave dug just deep enough to cover the coffin. The court and the people admired his enlightenment, and his family followed his instructions. (Jing's brother Xiaoxie served as a clerk in the Dali Temple and spoke to Lin. It was also said by people in the state.)

Wei Zhonggui, an official in the county, was known for his innate filial piety and respect for elders, and he was greatly respected by the local community. At the age of seventeen, the county recommended Zuo Yi to be the magistrate of Mengyang. Wei Zhonggui's father, named Lianmu, served as the deputy magistrate of Ziyang and retired due to old age. In the early Tang Dynasty, Lianmu fell ill, and Wei Zhonggui cared for him without removing his official attire, showing his dedication. After a long time, Lianmu passed away, and Wei Zhonggui dismissed his wives and concubines, guarding the tomb on the left side. He believed in Buddhism and recited the Lotus Sutra. During the day, he carried soil to create a boundary, and at night, he focused on reciting scriptures. He was diligent and tireless in this practice, remaining for three years without returning home. Once, a tiger came to the wall at night, crouching and listening to the recitation, staying for a long time without leaving. Wei Zhonggui, not wishing for communication with wild animals, got up and left. The next morning, he saw a bird like a duck, carrying two carp, which it placed on the ground and then flew away. The officials, deeply moved, reported the discovery of the lingzhi mushrooms to the state and county. At that time, Xin was the governor, and Shen Yu was the deputy governor, and they all went to the tomb to investigate. Suddenly, a bird like a duck appeared, carrying two carp, which it placed on the ground and then flew away. The officials, deeply moved, reported the discovery of the lingzhi mushrooms to the imperial court, and an imperial decree was issued to honor the family with a tablet at the gate.

A Man from Jiangdu Named Sun Bao Sun Bao was originally from the north. At the end of the Sui Dynasty, he moved to Jiangdu. When he was young, he died, but his body remained warm. After over forty days, he revived. He recounted his experience: "Initially, I was captured and brought to the government quarters. Suddenly, I saw my mother inside, under arrest. I was filled with both sorrow and joy. My mother then spoke, saying that since her death, she had been detained for a long time without any resolution or way to appeal. The next morning, the official in charge brought me before the magistrate. The magistrate said, 'You are released without blame.' I then asked, 'Unclear, is there a definite retribution for sins and merits in life?' The magistrate replied, 'There is.' I asked again, 'Can sins and merits be offset against each other?' The magistrate said, 'They can.' I said, 'My neighbor, Mr. A, has more sins than merits in life. He is now outside. My mother has more merits than sins, yet she has been detained for so long. If there is a definite retribution, why is this so?' The magistrate summoned the head clerk to ask. The clerk said there was no record. The magistrate then called my mother for questioning and found that she indeed had more merits than sins. He blamed the clerk for losing the case file and not knowing the details of the case. The official then reviewed the records."

According to what he said, he was ordered to be released and matched with the mother and son of the Music Hall. But the treasure was sent to a place of birth. The Music Hall was like a splendid palace, with grand halls and pavilions. Men and women enjoyed themselves there. The treasure had no intention of returning, but only toured and played in the various halls. About a month later, he encountered his elder brother on the road. The elder brother scolded him, saying, "You were not yet destined to die. Why didn't you return early?" The treasure replied, "I do not wish to return." The elder brother angrily said, "When people die, they receive their retribution based on their karma. Your karma is evil, and you cannot be born into the Music Hall. But because you were not yet destined to die, you could only be a guest there. If you die, the officials will take you in. How could you see your mother?" He then poured water from a bottle over the treasure, from head to toe, completely drenching his body. Only a little bit on his arm was not fully drenched, and the water ran out. He pointed to an empty room and ordered the treasure to enter it. After entering, he revived. The place where the water did not fully drench him, the flesh then rotted and fell off. Until today, only bones can be seen. (During the seventh year of the Zhen Guan era, I was sent as an envoy to the east, and Zhen Tuo told me this. The treasure is still alive.)

Zhang Liang was the highest-ranking official in the Duzhufu (都督府) of Youzhou. He was devoutly devoted to Buddhism. Once, he entered a temple and saw a statue of the Buddha that was as tall as himself. Zhang Liang treated the statue with special reverence. Later, while sitting in the hall, two maids stood beside him. Suddenly, he heard thunder and lightning. Zhang Liang had a natural fear of thunder, so he focused his mind on the images of the Buddha. Soon after, a loud thunderclap shook the hall's pillars. One of the maids ran out and died at the steps. A piece of wood struck Zhang Liang's forehead, but it did not hurt much. When he looked, there was only a red mark. The wooden pillar split in half and fell to the ground, as if it had been broken by a person. Later, Zhang Liang went to the temple and found a large mark on the back of the Buddha's head, as if it had been struck by something. The mark matched the place where Zhang Liang's mark was. Zhang Liang and the others were shocked and sighed in amazement (Zhang Liang himself told this story to Gao Yu, and it was also known in Youzhou). The censor from Fanyang, Lu Wenli, was initially the magistrate of Yunyang. He served as an envoy to the road to Jingzhou, covering prisoners, and traveled to Jiangnan. He encountered a very serious illness, with his abdomen swelling like a stone. He could not eat or drink, and medicines did not cure him. Lu Wenli thought that death was inevitable and had no hope of survival. He then focused his mind on the Bodhisattva Guanyin. After several days, he felt drowsy and dreamy. Suddenly, he saw a monk come to him and said that he was the Bodhisattva Guanyin. The monk said to Lu Wenli, "Since you can focus your mind, I have come to save you. Now, I will remove the illness from your abdomen." The monk held a piece of wood and used it to treat his abdomen. Three liters or more of foul matter came out of his abdomen, which was extremely foul-smelling. He felt better. He woke up in shock and found that his body and abdomen felt normal. He could eat and get up, and all his chronic illnesses were cured. He is still very strong. Bao and Jian both served as censors. They said this themselves.

Muxi Ren, a person from Zhaojun and Handan, was young and knowledgeable in the classics but did not believe in ghosts and spirits. He wanted to test the existence or non-existence of ghosts and went to seek knowledge from someone. After more than ten years, he could not see them. Later, he moved his family to a county and saw a person on the road who looked like a high-ranking official, dressed in very grand attire, riding a fine horse, accompanied by more than fifty riders. The person looked at Ren without speaking. After several times, the person still looked at Ren without speaking. The person was like this for ten years, meeting many times. Later, the person suddenly stopped the horse and called Ren, saying, "I have often seen you. I feel affectionate and admire each other. I want to be friends with you." Ren asked who the person was. The person replied, "I am a ghost. My surname is Cheng, and my name is Jing. Originally from Hongnong, during the Western Jin period, I served as an assistant governor. Now I serve as a high official in the country of Linhu." Ren asked where the country was and what the king's name was. The person replied, "North of the Yellow River, all is the country of Linhu. The capital is in the northwest of Loufan, it is the state of Zhouqi. The king is the former King Wuling of Zhao, now rules this country."

Under the jurisdiction of Mount Tai, each month the officials and representatives would ascend to pay homage at Mount Tai. Thus, I often pass through this place, and here I met you. I can indeed be beneficial to you. To allow you to foresee disasters and avoid them beforehand, you can avoid unexpected harm. Only matters of life and death, and great calamities and blessings, cannot be altered. Xian followed my advice. Jing then ordered his attendant to always manage affairs and to be a constant companion. He gave this attendant to follow Xian, to report matters first. If there is anything you don't know, come tell me. After this, they parted ways. The attendant always followed like a retainer, and recently asked questions, nothing was unknown beforehand. At the beginning of the Sui Dynasty, Cen Zhi Xiang of Jiangling was the magistrate of Handan. His son Wenben, who had not yet reached adulthood, Zhi Xiang invited Ren Xian to his home to teach Wenben. Xian recounted this matter to Wenben, saying, "The grown-up history says, 'I have something to say, but it is embarrassing to you.' Since we are friends, I cannot keep it from you." Even in the realm of spirits and deities, there is food.

Yet, they could not be satisfied. They suffered from hunger. If they could obtain human food, they could be full for a year. Many ghosts often stole human food. Being valuable, I could not steal it. I asked you for a meal. Xian informed Wenben, and Wenben then prepared the meal, arranging exquisite delicacies. Xian said, "Ghosts do not want to enter human houses. They can be set up outside, with a tent and a table, and wine and food placed on it." Wenben followed his words. At the time, Ren Xian saw two guests arrive and sit down, accompanied by over a hundred riders. After sitting, Wenben bowed twice toward the table, apologized for the food not being refined, also conveyed the meaning, and expressed gratitude. Initially, Wenben was going to prepare the food, and Ren Xian asked for gold and silk to redeem it. Wenben asked what kind of things they were. Xian explained that things used by ghosts differ from those used by humans, only gold and silk can be used universally, but they are not as good as fake ones, by coating large tin with yellow paint to make gold, and using paper to make silk, which are the most valuable. Wenben followed his instructions and made them, and when the guests had finished eating.

Let his followers take turns sitting and eating. Wenben gave him the money and silk he had made. Jing deeply thanked and said, "Because of the relationship with Muxing, it troubled you to provide for me." Do you wish to know your lifespan? Wenben replied, "I do not wish to know." Jing took his leave. Several years later, Ren Qian fell ill but was not severely troubled, and yet could not get up. After more than a month, Qian asked the usual attendant, who said he did not know. Then he asked the Grand Historian, who reported, "No one in the country knew." Later, because of the court in Tai Shan, to report the news. By the following month, the Grand Historian came to report, "It was your fellow townsman Zhao Wu who became the Chief Clerk of Tai Shan. There was a vacancy for the Chief Clerk. He recommended you for this position. Therefore, the documents were processed to summon you. Those who completed the case would die." Qian asked what plan would be taken. Jing said, "Your lifespan should be around sixty. You are now forty. But because Zhao, the Chief Clerk, arbitrarily summoned you, I will plead for you." Then said that Zhao, the Chief Clerk, asked, "Muxing, your elder brother, studied with you in the past. The bond of friendship was deep."

Now I am fortunate to have become the Chief Clerk of Tai Mountain. Just happened to encounter a vacancy for the Chief Clerk. The governor is now selecting someone. I have already informed the governor. The governor has agreed to use me. Brother, you cannot achieve immortality. You will eventually die. When you die, you may encounter opportunities. You may not necessarily get an official position. Why be reluctant to give up a few decades? If you are greedy for life. Now the official document has been issued. It cannot be stopped now. I hope you can make a decision about coming. Without any doubt. Qian is worried and fearful. The illness becomes more severe. Jing told Qian. Zhao Chief Clerk wants to bring you. You can come from Tai Mountain. To the governor for a complaint. Then you can avoid it. Qian asked. How can I see the governor? Jing said that only ghosts can be seen. Go to the Tai Mountain Temple. Cross a small hill to the east. The hill is level ground. This is their capital. You will naturally see it. Qian told Wenben. Wenben prepared the necessary travel arrangements. Several days later, Jing came again to tell Qian. The official document is about to be finalized. You should be afraid of making a complaint. Quickly get a Buddha statue. That way, the official document will naturally be resolved. Qian told Wenben.

Xian spent three thousand coins to have a painting of an image made on the west wall of the temple. Later, Jing came to inform that it was exempted or free. Xian, who did not believe in Buddhism, still had doubts. He then asked Jing, "According to Buddhist teachings, there is cause and effect across three lifetimes. Is this true or false?" Jing replied, "True." Xian said, "Just as people die, they will be divided into the six realms. How can all of them become ghosts? And Sui Wuling and you are still ghosts?" Jing said, "How many households are within your county?" Xian said, "More than ten thousand households." Jing asked again, "How many prisoners are there?" Xian said, "Usually no more than twenty people." Jing continued, "Within ten thousand households, how many officials of the fifth rank are there?" Xian said, "None." Jing asked, "How many officials of the ninth rank and above are there?" Xian said, "Dozens of people." Jing said, "Within the six realms, it is also like this. They get into the six realms, but not a single person. Like within your county, not a single official of the fifth rank, those who obtain the human realm have several people. Like you, officials of the ninth rank, those who enter hell also have dozens. Like within your prison, prisoners, only ghosts and animals."

The most numerous are the households within the county that are subject to taxation and labor duties. In this context, there are also hierarchical distinctions. He pointed to his attendants and said, "That person is not as good as I am, and those who are inferior to that person are even more numerous." Qian asked, "Do ghosts die?" The answer was yes. Qian asked, "Which path do they enter after death?" The response was unknown. Just as people know about death but do not know what happens after death, Qian asked, "Are Daoist rituals beneficial?" Jing replied, "The Dao is overseen by the Heavenly Emperor, who presides over the Six Paths, which is known as the celestial bureaucracy. The King of Hell is like a human emperor, and the Lord of Mount Tai, who is a high-ranking official, records the Five Path deities, similar to various high-ranking officials. If we were to consider our own country, it would be like a large state or prefecture. For each matter concerning the human world, petitions are submitted to the Dao for blessings. The celestial bureaucracy receives them and informs the King of Hell, saying, 'On a certain day, a certain person named A makes a complaint, etc.' It should be handled properly without causing injustice. The King of Hell respectfully receives and carries out the instructions, just as a person obeys an imperial decree. There is no way to avoid it if there is no reason, and if there is injustice, it must be rectified. It can be considered beneficial."

He then asked, "What is the nature of merit-making in Buddhism?" Jing replied, "Buddha is a great sage, and there are no written regulations. In terms of merit-making, he is revered by heavenly deities and often receives leniency. If one has accumulated great merit, even if there are records of bad deeds, they cannot be pursued. This is beyond my knowledge, and I also do not know why." After speaking, he left. Qian said after a short time that the patient could stand and recover. Wenben's father had died, and he returned to his hometown. Qian sent a letter saying, "Ghosts and spirits are definitely greedy and flattering. In the past, they wanted the gentleman to eat and drink, and were so diligent. Now they know there is no more profit, and the meeting is very cold. Although they still manage affairs, they still follow." The county was invaded by bandits, and almost everyone died. I was protected by the person in charge and was always like the bandits did not see me, and I finally managed to survive. On the ninth day of the ninth month in the sixteenth year of the Zhenguan era, officials were given recognition at the Northern Gate of Xuanwu. Wenben was serving as the Secretary of the Central Secretariat, with his elder brother, the Minister of the Imperial Household, and the Censor-in-Chief Ma Zhou, the Censor-in-Chief Wei Kun, and the Minister of the Imperial Household. Wenben said to all of them, "Thus."

Then someone asked again, "How does one cultivate merit in Buddhism?" Jing replied, "The Buddha is a great sage, who does not issue any decrees or documents. Those who cultivate merit are revered by celestial beings and often receive leniency. If one's merit is abundant, even if there are records of transgressions that would lead to rebirth in the lower realms, they cannot be pursued or enforced. This is beyond my knowledge, and I do not know the reason why." Having finished speaking, he departed. Qian said, "If you can rise, you will recover." Wenben’s father passed away, and he returned to his hometown. Qian sent a letter saying, "The ghosts and spirits are surely greedy and deceitful. Previously, they wanted you to eat and drink, so they were so diligent. Now that they know there is no more substantial benefit, they are especially indifferent. However, they still follow me around. Our county was overrun by bandits, and nearly everyone died. I was spoken of by the officials, and it was as if the bandits could not see me. In the end, I was saved." On the ninth day of the ninth lunar month in the sixteenth year of the Zhen Guan era, a civil official inscribed this record at the Northern Gate of Xuanwu. At that time, Wenben was serving as Councillor of the Secretariat, and was present with my elder brother, the Director of the Treasury, as well as Inspector of Legal Affairs Ma Zhou, Censor-in-Attendance Wei Kun, and others at the gathering. Wenben himself spoke these words to the others.

Attendant Physician Sun Huipu of the Palace. He was from Jiyin. In the thirteenth year of the Zhenguan era, he followed the imperial carriage to the Nine Cities Palace. Three Good Father and Wei Grand Tutor were neighbors. Once at the second watch of the night, he heard someone calling the name of the attendant physician outside the door. Sun went out to see, thinking it was the Grand Tutor's command. After going out, he saw two people who told Sun, "The official is calling." Sun replied, "I cannot walk." They then took Sun's horse and rode it. Following the two people, he felt as if the world was as bright as day. Sun was surprised but dared not speak. The two led Sun out of the valley, passing by the east of the court, then northeast for six or seven li, reaching the Alfalfa Valley. From afar, he saw two people guiding Han Feng to walk. They told the two who had led Sun, "You are mistaken. What I have is correct. You should release that person." They then released Sun. Sun followed the road back, finding it unlike anything he had experienced in life. After arriving home, he tied the horse, saw a maid sleeping by the door, called her but she did not respond. He stepped over and entered the house, seeing her body lying beside the wife. He wanted to approach her but could not, so he stood by the south wall, shouting loudly to the wife, who finally did not respond. The room was extremely bright, and he saw a spider web in the corner of the wall, with two flies, one large and one small. He saw the medicine on the beam, all clearly visible, but could not approach the bed. Knowing it was death, he was very worried and sad, wanting to say goodbye to his wife but could not. He leaned against the south wall, after a while fell into a light sleep, and suddenly woke up in a start, feeling himself lying on the bed, but the room was dark and nothing could be seen. He called out to the wife, told her to light a fire, and broke out in a sweat. Getting up and looking at the spider web, it was still clearly visible. He saw the horse also sweating. Han Feng died suddenly that night.

In the seventeenth year, Pu was ordered to travel by express post. Prince Qi returned home due to illness and arrived at the East Xiangyi Station in Luoyang. Suddenly, a person came and asked, "Are you Sun Hui Pu?" Pu replied, "Yes, I am." The person then asked, "Why are you here?" Pu responded, "I am just a ghost. Wei Taishi has a document summoning you to be his secretary." The ghost showed Pu the document, which was signed by Duke Zheng Wei Zheng. Pu was shocked and said, "Duke Zheng is not dead. Why is he sending you to deliver the message?" The ghost replied, "He is dead now. He is the Grand Administrator of the Solar Capital. That is why he has sent me to summon you." Pu invited the ghost to eat with him, and the ghost was very pleased and thanked Pu. Pu then said, "I have not yet returned from my official mission. It would be inappropriate for Duke Zheng to summon me. I will return to the capital after I have completed my duties and then obey his command. Is that acceptable?" The ghost agreed. From then on, they traveled together during the day and shared the same accommodation at night. They eventually reached Wenxiang. The ghost said, "I will go ahead to get the necessary documents for passing through the checkpoint and wait for you." Pu passed through the checkpoint and exited the western gate, where he saw the ghost waiting outside. They continued their journey together and arrived at Zishui Station. The ghost said to Pu, "Wait until you have finished your duties and we will meet again. Do not eat spicy or strong-smelling foods." Pu agreed. After completing his duties, Pu learned that Duke Zheng had died. Upon checking the date of his death, it was found to be the same day as the arrival at Xiangyi Station. Pu thought he would surely die and bid farewell to his family. He also invited monks to recite scriptures, create statues, and write sutras for about six or seven days. One night, he dreamed that the previous ghost came to summon him. The ghost led Pu up a high mountain, where there was a large palace on the mountain. Upon entering, Pu saw many gentlemen who welcomed him and said, "This person has done good deeds and cannot be kept here. Let him go." They then pushed Pu off the mountain. Pu woke up in shock and has since remained unharmed. Pu later recounted this story himself.

In the year of the Zhenguan era, the Minister of Revenue, Duke Wuchang Dai Wei, had always been on good terms with Shen Yu, the Deputy Governor of Shuzhou. Dai Wei passed away in the seventh year of the Zhenguan era. By the eighth year, in the month of August, Shen Yu was in his post when he had a dream. In the dream, he saw himself walking on the southern street of Yining Li in the capital. Suddenly, he saw Dai Wei, who was wearing old and tattered clothes and looked very weary. Seeing Shen Yu, Dai Wei showed both sadness and joy. Shen Yu asked, "What are you doing now, given your past good deeds?" Dai Wei replied, "When I was alive, I arranged for someone to be executed. After my death, someone slaughtered a sheep to offer as a sacrifice to me. These two matters have made the process of explaining and defending myself extremely difficult to describe. However, the situation is now clear. I also regret that I was unable to advance your position, which deeply troubled me. Now that you have achieved a fifth-rank position, and the official documents have passed through the celestial bureaucracy, I share in your joy and celebration, so I am telling you this as a way of informing you." After speaking, he woke up. He told others about the dream, hoping it would come true. That winter, Shen Yu went to the capital for the selection process but was punished for a copper-related offense and thus not eligible for an official position. He told others that his dream had not come true. In the spring of the ninth year, Shen Yu was about to return to Jiangnan. On his way to Xuzhou, he suddenly received an imperial edict, appointing him as a fifth-rank official and deputy governor of Wuzhou. (His brother, who served as a Ministry of Personnel official, heard about it and summoned Shen Yu to ask him about it.)

Minister of Revenue and Lord of Wuchang, Dai Wei, had long been on good terms with Shen Yu, the Deputy Governor of Shuzhou. Dai Wei died in the seventh year of the Zhenguan era. By August of the eighth year, while Shen Yu was still in the state, he dreamed that he was walking on the south street of Yining District in the capital. Suddenly, he saw Dai Wei, who was wearing old, tattered clothes and appeared very haggard. Seeing Shen Yu, Dai Wei was filled with both sorrow and joy. Shen Yu asked, "Throughout your life, you cultivated virtue. What are you doing now?" Dai Wei replied, "In my lifetime, I once submitted a memorial recommending the execution of one person. After my death, others slaughtered a sheep to offer in my honor. Because of these two matters, the toil of defending and explaining has been indescribable, yet now it is finally settled." He then said to Shen Yu, "Throughout my life, I have been friendly with you, yet I could not advance your official rank, which I deeply regret. Now you have attained the rank of fifth grade; your official documents have already passed through the Celestial Bureau. I rejoice with you in this shared joy, so I am reporting this to you as a way of repaying you." After saying this, he awoke. He told others about the dream, hoping it would be verified. That winter, Shen Yu went to the capital for the selection process, but due to a copper penalty, he was not granted an official post. He then told others that the dream had not come true. In the spring of the ninth year, Shen Yu was about to return to Jiangnan, and on his way to Xuzhou, he suddenly received an imperial edict appointing him to the fifth grade, as Administrator of Wuzhou (his elder brother, who was the Minister of Personnel, heard of this and summoned Shen Yu to inquire about it).

During the reign of Emperor Gaozu in the early Tang Dynasty, Li Da'an, the elder brother of Li Da-liang, who was the Minister of Works, traveled from the capital to visit his brother. Li Da-liang sent several servants and maidservants to accompany their brother on his journey. They reached Luoyang and stayed at an inn in Guzhou's Lu Bridge. One of the servants had plotted to kill Li Da'an. When Li Da'an fell asleep, the servant waited until he was unconscious, then stabbed him in the neck with a small sword, piercing him deeply. The blade became stuck in the bed. The servant then walked around the room. Li Da'an suddenly woke up, calling out to the servant. The loyal servants, seeing the danger, tried to pull the blade out. Li Da'an said, "If you pull out the blade, I will die. Instead, get some paper and ink and write a letter." The servants then went to inform the innkeeper and reported the incident to the local authorities. After writing the letter, the county official arrived and helped to remove the blade, wash the wound, and apply medicine. Li Da'an then passed away. Suddenly, as if in a dream, he saw an object about a foot long, four to five inches wide and thick, shaped like pork, hovering about two feet above the ground and moving toward the bed. A voice from within the object said, "Hurry and return my meat." Li Da'an replied, "I do not eat pork. How could I owe you anything?" They heard someone outside say, "That's wrong." The object then returned and went out through the door. Li Da'an then saw a pond of water in front of the courtyard, clear and shallow, lovely to look at. On the western bank of the pond, there was a gold statue of Buddha, about five inches tall.

In a short while, Dahan gradually grew larger and transformed into a monk. The monk wore a new and clean robe with a border. He asked Dahan, "Are you injured? I will take away your pain. You should recover, return home, and chant Buddha's name and do good deeds." Then he touched the back of Dahan's neck and left. Dahan remembered the monk's appearance, noting that the back of the monk's robe had a red patch, about the size of a square inch, very clearly visible. Soon after, Dahan revived and the wound on his neck no longer hurt. He was able to sit up and eat. Several days later, the children of officials in the capital welcomed him back home. Family members came to see him, and Dahan explained the cause of his injury and what he had seen of the monk. A woman who was present listened and said, "When Dahan first went out, his wife sent a maid to a sculptor to make a statue of Dahan. The statue was completed, and the robe was inscribed with blue writing. There was a dot of red on the back. When they sent the sculptor to remove it, he refused, and it is still there. The shape is as described by the young master." Dahan then went with his wife and family to view the statue, and it was indeed what he had seen. The red dot on the back of the robe was clearly in the patch. They were amazed, and Dahan then came to revere and believe in Buddhism. Dahan's wife, Lady Xiahou, is the daughter of the governor Xuan. Previously, she had told the governor. Later, Dahan's nephew, Su, became the Chief Justice and also said so.

During the reign of Emperor Gaozu in the early Tang Dynasty, Su Chang, the Water Management Commissioner, was appointed as the Prefect of Yi Prefecture. Su Chang took his family with him to assume his new post, crossing the Jialing River. In midstream, a sudden wind arose, causing the boat to capsize. More than sixty men and women drowned at once. Only one concubine survived; she had always recited the Lotus Sutra. When the boat sank, she placed the sutra on her head and vowed to drown with it. However, she alone did not drown, floating with the waves until she washed ashore. She eventually reached Yang Prefecture, where she married and became even more devout in her faith. According to Cen Shuo, she herself claimed that she had once been on the Jialing River, and the boatmen also recounted the same story. In Hedong, Dong Xiong was a young man who sincerely believed in Buddhism and Daoism. He had practiced vegetarianism for several decades. During the reign of Emperor Taizong, he served as a Deputy Justice of the Dali Court. In the spring of the fourteenth year of his reign, he was implicated in the case of Lian Jixian's servant and was imprisoned in the Office of Censorship. At the time, the emperor was expected to be furious, and the Chief Justice, Wei, was sent to interrogate him harshly. The prison was tightly guarded by more than ten officials. Deputy Justice Li Jingxuan and Inspector Wang Xin were also implicated in the case and were imprisoned in the same room as Dong Xiong, both of them locked securely. Dong Xiong devoted himself to reciting the *Lotus Sutra* and the *Universal Gateway Chapter*. In a few days, he had recited it three thousand times. One night, as he sat alone reciting the sutra, the lock suddenly fell off by itself. Surprised, Dong Xiong told Wang and Li about it. Wang and Li examined the lock and found that it was still firmly attached to the ground, with its hooks intact and undisturbed, yet the lock and hooks were several feet apart. They were amazed by this. Fearing punishment, Dong Xiong asked the guards to open the lock. The night watchman, Zhang Jingyi, ordered a clerk to open the lock. When the clerk inspected it with a candle, he saw that the hooks and lock were not connected but had naturally separated. He found this very strange and relocked it, sealing the lock with paper and writing his name on it. The clerk then left.

Xiong continued to recite scriptures until the fifth watch of the night. The lock fell and made a sound, as if someone had opened it. Xiong, fearing this, told Xin Xuanxuan and others that it was not advisable to summon officials in the morning. When morning came, they all examined it together. The hooks and locks were separated on the ground, but the lock remained closed. The sealed and marked areas remained untouched. The hooks were very secure and tightly fastened, making it impossible to open them by normal means. Xin Xuanxuan, who had never believed in Buddhism since his youth, often said to his wife when he saw her reading scriptures, "Why do you worship foreign deities? Are you flattering them by reading these texts?" Upon witnessing this event involving Xiong, he deeply realized and exclaimed, "I now understand that the Buddha is the greatest saint, unparalleled in his greatness. His power is truly beyond comprehension." At that time, Xin Xuanxuan also recited the names of the Eight Bodhisattvas three thousand times. When he finished, the locks fell and released themselves completely. Upon examination, the locks appeared the same as those of Xiong's case. Xin Xuanxuan thus became convinced, felt ashamed, and repented. Shortly after, the three sons were exonerated. Xin Xuanxuan then wrote the Lotus Sutra and drew images of the Eight Bodhisattvas, offering them for worship. (When he was critically ill at home, Xin Xuanxuan came to ask about his condition. He detailed the entire story. When he recovered and resumed his duties, he questioned the officials within the government. Their accounts matched those given by Xin Xuanxuan. Xiong also recounted the same story, and his dedication and practice became even more intense. Xiong is currently serving as the magistrate of Zhiuli.)

During the Wu De period, Su Chang, the Inspector of Waterways, was appointed as the Prefect of Yi Prefecture. Su Chang, along with his family, set out to take office. As they crossed the Jialing River, a sudden storm arose in midstream, causing the boat to sink. More than sixty men and women drowned at the same time. Only one concubine, who often read the Lotus Sutra, managed to survive. The river water entered the boat, and the concubine placed the scripture case on her head, swearing to go down with it. After the boat sank, the concubine alone did not sink, drifting with the waves. Soon, she reached the shore, then carried the scripture case, and opened to look at the scripture, which was completely dry and unsoiled. Now, she still exists in Yangzhou, having married and become a wife, and has become even more devout. According to the account by Cen Lingshuo, who recorded this event, the concubine herself claimed it happened as described, and the boatmen on the river also confirmed the story.

Cen Wenben, the Secretary of the Central Secretariat, was from Jingling.
