When he was young, he believed in Buddhism and often recited the *Saddharma Puṇḍarīka Sūtra* (Lotus Sutra) and the *Universal Gate Chapter*. Once, he traveled by boat on the Wujiang River, but the boat broke in midstream, and everyone aboard died. Cen Wenben was in the water when he heard someone say, "Just recite the Buddha's name, and you will definitely not die." This was repeated three times. Soon after, he rose with the waves and found himself on the northern shore, thus escaping death. Later, in Jingling, he held a vegetarian meal, and monks gathered at his home. A guest monk left last and told Cen Wenben, "The world is in chaos, but you are fortunate not to be like others. You will eventually encounter peace and prosperity." After finishing speaking, the monk hurriedly left. Soon after, Cen Wenben found two relics in his own bowl. Later, as he said, everything turned out as predicted. Yuan Dabao of Henan served as the Chief Justice during the Zhenguan era. He never believed in the doctrine of cause and effect. He was friends with his colleague Zhang Sance, and they often said, "If one of us dies first, we will come back to report whether there is a cause and effect." Yuan died in the eleventh year, accompanying the emperor to Luoyang and dying of illness. Zhang Sance in the capital did not know this. One night, Zhang had a dream where Yuan came to say, "I have already died. I never believed in the reward for good and evil, but now it is definitely true. So I came to report. You should diligently cultivate good deeds." Zhang asked about the situation, and Yuan replied, "The cause and effect in the afterlife cannot be explained, and others cannot describe it. Just let you know it definitely exists." Zhang woke up and told his colleagues. Two days later, the news of Yuan's death arrived. Zhang verified that the dream occurred after Yuan's death.

A Military Officer of the Right Gate of the Eastern Palace named Zheng Shiban. When he was still young, not yet having reached the age of twenty, he died from a strange illness. After three days, he revived. He recounted that several people were taken and were about to enter the main gate of an official government building. There were over a hundred prisoners there, all standing in rows facing north. There were a total of six rows. Those in the front row were fat and white in appearance, dressed well, and resembled nobles. Those in the back rows gradually became thinner and uglier, some wearing shackles, others just without headbands and belts. All were walking with linked sleeves, guarded by strict soldiers. When Zheng Shiban arrived, he was assigned to the third position from the east in the third row. He too was without headbands and belts, walking with linked sleeves. Feeling fearful, he focused on reciting Buddha's name. Suddenly, he saw a monk he knew from before come in. The monk entered the line of prisoners surrounded by soldiers, and the soldiers did not stop him. The monk then went to Zheng Shiban and said to him, "In your life, you did not cultivate merit. Now, how will you handle being here?" Zheng Shiban begged for mercy and asked for help. The monk said, "I will now save you if you keep the precepts." Zheng Shiban agreed. In a moment, an official led in all the prisoners and asked each one in order until reaching Zheng Shiban. The monk then saw Zheng Shiban and spoke to the official about his merits. The official said to release him. The monk then led Zheng Shiban out to the door, gave him the Five Precepts, poured water from a bottle over his forehead, and said to him, "You will live in the west."

A yellow shawl was given to Bian, and he was told, "Wear this to get home and place it in a clean place. I will also show you the way back." Bian wore the shawl and returned home. Upon arriving, he folded the shawl and placed it at the corner of the bed. Soon after, his eyes opened and his body moved. The family members were startled and scattered, thinking he was coming back to life. Only his mother stayed, asking, "Are you well?" Bian replied, "On the west side, my intention will be fulfilled." At that time, there was doubt about the timing, and his mother asked again. She said, "It was midnight." It was then known that the reversal of life and death occurred during the night. When Bian reached the west side, he could eat and recovered. He still saw the shawl at the head of the bed. When Bian could stand up, the shape of the shawl gradually faded but still had a glow. After seven days, it completely disappeared. Bian then kept the Five Precepts. Several years later, someone persuaded him to eat pork. Bian, unable to refuse, ate a small piece. That night, he had a dream in which he had transformed into a Rakshasa, with claws and teeth each several feet long, catching and eating a live pig. In the morning, he woke up feeling a fishy taste in his mouth and spitting out blood. When others looked inside, his mouth was full of clotted blood. Bian was terrified and dared not eat meat anymore. Another few years passed, and he married. His wife forced him to eat meat, but it had no effect. However, for the past six years, Bian has had a large, swollen sore on his nose that has not healed. It is feared that this might be due to breaking his precepts. (Linxie and Bian served together in the Eastern Palace and Bian told him himself.)

The Lady Doulu, the mother of Chen Gong, was the sister of Rui Gong Kuan. She was devoted to good fortune and recited the Diamond Sutra. She had not yet finished reading the entire scroll, only about one sheet, and had been unable to complete it for a long time. Later, one evening, she suffered from a headache and felt uneasy all over. Her condition worsened at night when she lay down to sleep. The lady thought to herself, "If I die, I will not be able to finish the sutra." She wanted to get up and recite it, but the candle in the hall had already gone out. The lady then ordered the maid to light a candle. After a short while, the maid returned and reported that there was no fire in the kitchen. The lady ordered to open the door and went to someone's house to ask for fire, but there was still no fire. The lady deeply sighed with regret. Suddenly, she saw a burning candle in the courtyard. It came up the steps and entered the hall, reaching the bed, about three feet off the ground, but no one was holding it. It was as bright as day. The lady was surprised and delighted, and her headache also subsided. She immediately took the sutra and recited it. After a short while, the family obtained fire by drilling wood and lit a candle in the hall. The candlelight then went out. From this day on, she recited it five times daily as a routine. After Rui Gong was about to die, the lady went to see him. He said to her, "My sister, due to the blessings from reciting the sutra, lived to be a hundred years old and was born in a good place." The lady is still healthy and is already eighty years old. The lady herself told her younger sister-in-law about this.

Left Gatekeeper General Li Shanlong from Pingyi. In the Wu De era, he suddenly fell ill and died. However, his heart area remained warm as if the size of a palm. His family hesitated to prepare the body for burial. On the seventh day, he revived and recounted his experience: "When I was dying, I was captured by palace guards and taken to an official office. The hall was very grand, and the courtyard was also spacious. Inside the courtyard, there were thousands of prisoners, some in shackles and others in chains, all facing north standing in the courtyard. The officials led me to the hall. A high-ranking official sat on an elevated seat, surrounded by attendants like a king. I asked the official, 'What kind of official is this?' The official replied, 'He is a king.' I approached the steps and the king asked, 'What good deeds have you done in your life?' I answered, 'The locals often held gatherings for religious teachings, and I always contributed material support to these gatherings.' The king then asked, 'What good deeds have you personally done?' I said, 'I recited two volumes of the Lotus Sutra.' The king said, 'Great virtue. You may ascend the steps.' After ascending, there was an elevated seat in the northeast corner of the hall, like a lectern. The king pointed to the seat."

The king said to Shanlong, "You may ascend this seat to recite the sutra." Shanlong, following the command, approached the side of the seat. The king immediately stood up and said, "Please ascend the seat, Venerable Master." Shanlong ascended the seat and finished. The king then sat facing him. Shanlong began to recite, "The First Chapter of the Lotus Sutra." The king said, "Please stop, Venerable Master." Shanlong descended from the seat and stood at the foot of the steps. He looked around the courtyard and saw that all the prisoners had disappeared, leaving no one behind. The king said to Shanlong, "The merit of your reciting the sutra is not only for your own benefit but also caused all the prisoners in the courtyard to gain release as if they had heard the sutra. How good it is! Now I will let you return." Shanlong bowed and took leave, walking several steps. The king called him back and said to the official, "Take this person to view all the prisons." The official led Shanlong more than a hundred steps east, where they saw an iron city, very large and spacious, with a roof above it. There were many small windows on the side of the city, some as large as small bowls, others as large as cups or bowls. Shanlong saw men and women flying into the windows from the ground and did not come out again. Shanlong asked the official in surprise. The official said, "These are the realms of the hungry ghosts and the hell beings. They are born there due to their past misdeeds and remain trapped there, unable to escape. The recitation of the sutra by a virtuous person like you has the power to liberate them, even though they are not present here." Shanlong was deeply moved by this revelation and thanked the king for the opportunity to perform such a meritorious act. The king smiled and said, "The power of the Dharma is indeed wondrous. Continue to recite the sutra and spread the teachings, and you will bring great benefit to all beings." With these words, the king released Shanlong, who returned to his monastery, his heart filled with joy and a renewed sense of purpose.

This is a great prison. The prison has many divisions, and punishments vary. These people each follow their own profession, going to prison to suffer their punishment. Mountain Dragon heard of this and was filled with fear and sorrow. He chanted "Namo Buddha" and asked the official to let him out. Upon reaching the courtyard gate, he saw a large cauldron of boiling hot soup. Nearby, two people were sitting and sleeping. Mountain Dragon asked them, and they replied, "Our punishment is to enter this cauldron of boiling soup. We are blessed by the virtuous one chanting 'Namo Buddha,' so all the prisoners in the prison get one day of rest, tired and sleeping." Mountain Dragon chanted "Namo Buddha" again. The official told Mountain Dragon, "This office frequently changes. Now the king lets you go. You can report to the king and request a record. If not, other officials might not know and will pursue and record you again." Mountain Dragon immediately went to the king and requested a record. The king ordered paper and wrote one line of text, giving it to the official and saying, "To obtain signatures from the Five Departments." The official received the order and took Mountain Dragon through two departments, with various hall attendants present, and it was the same as before. The king sent the official to obtain their official seals, and each wrote one line and finished, giving it to Mountain Dragon. Dragon held it and went out to the gate.

Three people approached Shan Long and said, "The king is letting you go. Can you not ask for a little more to send us?" Shan Long remained silent. The official then told Shan Long, "The king is letting you go, not through you." The three people said, "These are those who previously captured you and were your handlers. One is the rope master, who should have tied you with a red rope; one is the stick master, who hit your head; one is the bag master, who sucked your breath. Seeing that you have returned, they are asking for something in return." Shan Long was terrified and thanked the three people, saying, "I am foolish and do not recognize you. Please come to my home to prepare things, but I don't know where to send them." The three people replied, "By the water or under a tree." Shan Long agreed, bid farewell to the official, and returned home. Upon arriving, he found himself crying and preparing funeral items. Shan Long entered and approached the body, and immediately revived. Later, he said, "Cut paper into money and silk, and prepare wine and food. I will personally take them to the water's edge and burn them." Suddenly, he saw the three people return and thank him, saying, "Thank you for not breaking your promise and for generously giving us more. We are grateful and ashamed." After they finished speaking, they disappeared. Shan Long then told the chief monk of Zongchi Temple and turned to Lin, recounting the story.

Records of Rebirth, Volume 2 During the late Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, there was a man from Dai State, surnamed Wang, who was appointed as a cavalry general and stationed in Jingzhou to guard the region. He had a fondness for hunting and killed many animals. He had five sons and no daughters. Later, he had a daughter who was as beautiful and graceful as a painting. Those who saw her all found her to be extraordinary, and her parents cherished and loved her deeply. After returning to his hometown, the villagers and relatives competed to make her good clothes and shared in her beauty. The girl was seven years old when one day she disappeared and no one knew where she was. At first, they suspected the neighbors were hiding her for fun, but after searching, no one saw her. Her brothers rode horses to search far away, going more than thirty miles from home. They saw her in the thorns and tried to approach and take her, but she immediately ran away in fear. The horse could not catch up. Her brothers, with ten riders, surrounded and captured her. She made a sound like a rabbit and was carried back home. She could not speak, but her body was injured by thorn pricks. Her mother picked out the thorns, removing many thorns. After a month or so, she died without eating. Her parents were heartbroken and the whole family fasted and prayed. The high-ranking official Cai Xuanming, who once served as a legal official in the Dai State, told me this.

Cui Hao, the Grand Tutor of the Northern Wei, was broadly learned and possessed strategic insight. He served Emperor Taiwu, and his words and actions were followed without question. The people regarded him as a model. Cui Hao served under the Taoist priest Guan Qianzhi, who particularly did not believe in Buddhism. He often spread falsehoods at the expense of the common people. Once, he saw his wife reading scriptures and took it away, throwing it into a well. Accompanying Emperor Taiwu to Chang'an, he entered a temple and found weapons (bows, arrows, swords, and spears) inside. The emperor was furious and ordered the execution of the monks. Cui Hao then advised, leading to the killing of all the monks, the burning of the scriptures and images, and the issuance of an edict from the command center. All regions followed the example of Chang'an. Guan Qianzhi argued with Cui Hao, but Cui Hao did not follow his advice. Guan Qianzhi told Cui Hao, "You will be punished from now on, and your family will be destroyed." Four years later, Cui Hao was indeed executed by the entire family despite being innocent. Before his execution, he was put on a cart, with ten officials present. They urinated in his mouth, and after traveling several miles, he could no longer bear the suffering and cried out in distress. He finally endured all five punishments. No such humiliation and punishment had been seen before in ancient times. The emperor also wrongly executed the crown prince. Soon after, Cui Hao was killed by the eunuch Zong Ai. At the time, people believed this was the retribution for destroying Buddhism. (See the *History of the Northern Wei* and *Ten Clouds Because of Spring and Autumn Annals*.)

**Emperor Yuan of Liang** was six years old when he saw a large pearl in his mother's box. He took one pearl and put it in his mouth, then accidentally swallowed it. The pearl was suspected to have been stolen by someone nearby. After questioning, no one admitted to it. He then used ashes and live fish to curse the pearl. The next day, the emperor sent an envoy, and the pearl followed the envoy out. The emperor's one eye became blind (see the later account of Liang). **Emperor Wu of Zhou** enjoyed eating chicken eggs and would eat several at one sitting. There was a supervisor of the imperial kitchen named Babi, who often served the emperor and was favored. When Emperor Wen of Sui ascended the throne, Babi continued to serve the imperial kitchen. During the Kaihuang era, Babi died suddenly, but his heart was still warm. His family was unwilling to bury him. After three days, he revived and could speak. He first said, "Raise me to see the king." He was to convey a message for Emperor Wu of Zhou. Then he requested to see the king. Emperor Wen of Sui led him to ask, and Babi said, "Suddenly, I saw someone calling me. I followed them to a place. There was a large underground cave. We walked along the path and entered the cave. As soon as we reached the entrance of the cave, I saw over a hundred riders coming from the west. They were guarded like a king. Soon they arrived at the cave entrance. It was Emperor Wu of Zhou. Babi bowed. The king said, 'The king called you. To prove my case. You have no guilt.' After saying this, he entered the cave. The messenger also led Babi into the palace through the palace gates and into the courtyard. He saw Emperor Wu of Zhou sitting with another person, and there was a guest treated with extra respect. The messenger ordered Babi to bow to the king. The king asked, 'How many white团 did you serve the emperor?' Babi did not recognize what white团 was."

The emperor looked around. Those nearby explained, "Chicken eggs are called 'white balls.'" The translator immediately replied, "The emperor often eats white balls, but he really doesn't like to count them." The king said to the emperor, "This person doesn't remember. They must be dismissed." The emperor looked sad and unwillingly got up. He quickly saw a courtyard before him, with an iron bed and dozens of prison guards, all with the heads of cows and the bodies of humans. The emperor was already lying on the bed, and the prison guards used iron beams to bind him. The emperor's ribs were cut open, and from the cuts, chicken eggs all came out. Soon, they filled up the bed, about ten dou. Then, they counted them all, and the bed and the prison disappeared. The emperor was again sitting with the king. The king told the translator to return. Someone led him out to the mouth of a cave. They also saw Emperor Wu coming out. He told the translator, "I heard that the Great Sui Emperor once worked with me. The treasures and valuables in the warehouse were also stored by me. Now, as an emperor, I destroyed Buddhism and suffered great suffering. You can do good deeds for me." Therefore, Emperor Wen issued an edict to the people of the world, asking them to contribute one coin each to seek blessings (my maternal grandfather, Prince Qi, personally witnessed this and told me upon returning home).

During the Northern Qi Dynasty, there was a scholar-official surnamed Liang, who was very wealthy and prosperous. As he lay on his deathbed, he told his wife and children, "Throughout my life, I have loved my servants and horses. They have all been used for a long time, to my satisfaction. After I die, they can be sacrificed to accompany me. Otherwise, there will be no one to ride them." When he died, his family stuffed the servant with earth and crushed him to death. The horse was not killed. The servant died and then revived after four days. He said, "I didn't feel myself being taken away. I hurried to the gate of the official residence. The gatekeeper detained me. I spent a night at the gate. The next morning, I saw my master being bound and guarded by armed soldiers. As he was being taken into the official residence, he saw the servant and said to him, 'After I died, the people obtained the slaves, and my last words were to call you. Now we are each suffering, but our names are completely unrelated. Now the official will release you.' After he finished speaking, he went in. The servant peered from behind the screen and saw the official ask the guard, 'How much oil was collected yesterday?' The guard replied, 'Eight sheng were obtained.' The official said, 'Take it away again.'

A certain amount of one shi and six dou was collected. The owner was immediately led out and could not speak. The next day, when he came again, he had a joyful expression. He said to the slave, "I will speak on your behalf today." When they entered, the official asked if they had obtained the fat. They replied that they had not. The official asked why. The owner said, "This person died three days ago. The family invited monks to set up a vegetarian meal. Whenever they heard the recitation of scriptures, the iron beam would break. Therefore, they could not obtain it." The official said, "For now, let us release the owner. I will also inform the official to release the slave." The official then called for the release, and they all went out. The owner sent a message to his wife and children, saying, "Thanks to you all for seeking blessings, I have been spared great suffering. However, I am still not free. If you can write the Lotus Sutra again, create statues, and offer them to the Buddhas, we may be able to be saved. From now on, do not set up sacrifices." Afterward, he felt that he had increased his own guilt. They parted after finishing speaking. The slave was born and told the story in detail. The family indeed held a gathering on that day and spent all their wealth to seek blessings, ordering the door practice (as told by my uncle Gao Jingzhou, who saw a story about a disaster in the state of Qi). During the Sui dynasty, the Duke of Pushe Mountain, Li Kuan, who held the title of Shangzhuguo, loved hunting and often kept dozens of eagles. Later, he had a son who was born with an eagle's beak, so he did not raise him (he was the father of Li Mi, and his neighbors and relatives all saw it).

During the Sui Dynasty, a woman named Jiao Lüo, who was a military officer, was skilled in hunting and falconry. Later, she fell ill and saw a group of thousands of birds, all without heads, circling around her bed. They cried out, "Quickly return our heads!" Jiao Lüo immediately suffered from a severe headache and lost consciousness. After a long time, she regained consciousness and prayed to perform good deeds for the birds. She was granted permission, and the birds all left. Soon after, she recovered and decided to abstain from meat and alcohol for the rest of her life, and she also refrained from killing any living beings. (This story was told to me by someone named Lin, who saw Jiao Lüo in Longyou during the summer when he was about sixty years old.) In the early years of the Kaihuang era of the Sui Dynasty, in a village outside Jizhou, there was a thirteen-year-old boy who often stole his neighbor's chicken eggs and roasted them to eat. One morning, before the villagers had risen, his father heard someone knocking on the door and calling out for the boy. His father sent the boy to answer the door and saw a man who said, "The official calls you in." The boy replied, "If it's to summon me for service, let me go inside and get my clothes and food." The messenger said, "No need." He then led the boy out of the village gate. The land south of the village was formerly mulberry fields, and it had been plowed but not yet sown. Suddenly, the boy saw a small city along the road, with four-sided gate towers that were very beautiful. The boy was surprised and asked, "When did this city appear?" The messenger scolded him and told him not to speak. He then led the boy to the north gate of the city and ordered him to enter. As soon as the boy entered, the city gate closed behind him. He saw no one, only an empty city, with the ground covered in ashes and broken coals, deep enough to cover his ankles. The boy suddenly cried out and ran toward the south gate, but it closed before he could reach it. He then ran to the east and north gates, but they closed as well.

Before going, it opens. After arriving, it closes. At that time, the villagers went out to the fields. Both men and women, young and old, all saw it. This boy was in the middle of the cultivated land, his mouth seemed like a crying sound, and he ran in all directions. They all said to each other, "Is this child crazy? And came like this, continuously playing." By the time of mealtime, those who picked mulberry leaves all returned. The boy's father asked, "Have you seen my son?" The mulberry pickers replied, "He is running and playing in the south of the village. When called, he wouldn't come." The father went out of the village, saw the boy running from afar, and loudly called out his name. At once, the boy stopped. Suddenly, the ashes disappeared, and he saw his father and fell down, crying without speaking. Looking at his foot, above the calf, the blood and flesh were burned dry, and below the knee, it was swollen and rotten like roasted meat. He was carried back to be cared for, and the thigh flesh remained as before, but below the knee became dry bones. The neighbors heard about it and together looked at the place where he had run. The footprints were smooth and unobstructed, with no traces of ashes or fire. Thus, the people of the town, both men and women, young and old, all took up the practice of self-cultivation and discipline (there was a great monk named Dao Hui from Jizhou, who explained this to the people of Lin Yan; this was their neighboring town).

During the Dàyè period of the Sui dynasty, a jailer in Jingzhao Prefecture, whose name has been lost to history, treated the prisoners cruelly and brutally, taking their suffering as a form of entertainment. Later, he gave birth to a son who had a jaw and shoulders as if a meaty collar, with no neck. The child lived for several years before dying unable to walk. During the Dàyè period, a woman from Henan was disrespectful to her mother-in-law, who was blind. The woman chopped earthworms into a soup to feed her. Her mother-in-law was surprised by the taste and secretly saved a piece to show to her child. When the child saw it, the woman wanted to take her to the county office. Before she could, a thunderstorm struck, and the woman disappeared. Soon after, she fell from the sky, still wearing the same clothes but with her head replaced by that of a white dog. Her speech remained unchanged. When asked why, she replied that she had been punished by the gods for her disrespect to her mother-in-law. She was taken to the county office, where she begged for food in the market. Her whereabouts after that are unknown. In the Yangzhou region, there was a man named Bian Shiyu, whose father had been granted the title of Yitong for his role in subduing Chen during the Sui dynasty. He was stingy and once hired workers to build his house without paying them. The workers sought payment, and Bian's father beat them. They all complained, saying, "If you truly owe us, you will die and become our cow." When Bian's father died, the workers' cow gave birth to a yellow calf with black markings around its waist, crisscrossing all around, like a human belt. On its left flank, there were white markings crossing diagonally, the size and shape exactly like a ministerial tablet. The owner of the cow called out, "Why did you, Sir Bian, owe me?" The calf knelt on its front legs and bowed its head to the ground. Bian Shiyu redeemed it with ten thousand coins, but the owner refused, and the calf was buried after its death (as recounted by Zhentuo of Lin).

Anren from Jingzhao, a man of great wealth, had always been devoted to the Buddhist monks at Cimen Temple. In the early years of the Yining era, a guest stayed at his house. The guest stole a donkey's skin and left it for Anren. By the third year of the Zhenguan era, Anren suddenly met someone on the road who told him, "The officials are chasing you. They will come tomorrow. You will die." Anren was terrified and went straight to the Buddha hall at Cimen Temple, where he stayed overnight without leaving. The next day at mealtime, indeed, there were three riders, and several dozen people armed with weapons entered the temple. They saw Anren from afar and called him out. Anren did not respond but was reciting Buddhist scriptures with even greater concentration. The ghost said to him, "We didn't take you yesterday. Now you are practicing virtue so diligently. How can we achieve this?" So they all left, leaving one person to guard. The guard told Anren, "You killed the donkey the other day. The donkey is now complaining about you. So we have come to capture you. In the end, you must face him. It won't help if you don't go." Anren replied from afar, "The thief killed the donkey himself and only left the skin for me. I did not kill it. Why am I being pursued? Please tell the donkey for me. I originally did not kill you. But now I am praying for you. It will be beneficial for you. You should let me go." This person agreed. If the donkey does not agree, we will come back tomorrow. If it agrees, we will not come back. After saying this, they left. The next day, they did not come. Anren then prayed for the donkey, and the whole family kept the precepts and ate vegetarian food. (Lu Wenli said that Anren is still alive today.)

In the marketplaces of Chang'an, there are certain customs. After the first day of the new year, people take turns preparing food and inviting each other, calling it "transmitting the seat." One day, a guest arrived at the home of a pen worker named Zhao Shici. Before the guest went to the toilet, he saw a young girl, about thirteen or fourteen years old, wearing a blue skirt and a white shirt, with a rope tied around her neck and attached to a mortar post. She wept and told the guest, "I am the master's daughter. Last year, before I died, I stole one hundred coins from my parents to buy cosmetics, but I did not get to use them before I died. Now the money is in the northwest corner of the kitchen wall. Although it was not used, it was already stolen, and for this, I am punished. Now I am paying back my parents' debt." After she finished speaking, she transformed into a blue sheep with a white neck. The guest was startled and told the master. The master asked about her appearance and recognized her as his little daughter, who had died two years ago. They sent the sheep to the kitchen wall and retrieved the money, which seemed to have been in a safe place for a long time. They then sent the sheep to a Buddhist temple, and the entire household stopped eating meat.

A Man from Jingzhao Named Pan Guo Pan Guo, a young man from Jingzhao, was not yet twenty years old. During the reign of Emperor Gaozu (Wude era), he served as a minor official in charge of water management. One day, he descended with several young men from the village to play in the fields. Passing by a tomb, they saw a sheep, abandoned by its owner, eating grass alone. Pan Guo and the young men caught the sheep, intending to take it home. The sheep began to bleat on the way, and fearing that its owner might hear, Pan Guo pulled out its tongue, silencing it. That night, they cooked and ate the sheep. A year later, Pan Guo's tongue began to shrink and eventually disappeared entirely. He submitted a petition to resign from his position. The county magistrate, Zheng Yuqing, suspected him of being dishonest. To test him, Zheng opened his mouth to inspect, finding that Pan Guo had no tongue. However, a small part of it, the size of a bean, remained. Zheng found this strange and questioned Pan Guo. Pan Guo told him everything that had happened. The county magistrate advised him to perform good deeds for the sheep's sake. Pan Guo then took the five precepts and performed many good deeds. A year later, his tongue gradually grew back and soon returned to normal. He went to the county office to report his recovery. The county magistrate appointed him as the village head. Zheng Yuqing, who was a censor during the eighteenth year of the Zhenguan era, recounted this story to me in Linzi.

During the reign of Emperor Yang of the Sui Dynasty, there was a man from Luoyang named Wang who observed the Five Precepts. He often spoke of things that had not yet happened, and the local people respected and trusted him. One day, he suddenly told someone, "Today, someone will come and give me a donkey. By noon, indeed, someone brought a donkey." The person cried and said, "I lost my father early, and my mother was widowed. She raised one son and one daughter. My sister married, and then my mother died, about ten years ago. On the Cold Food Festival, my sister came back home. We had a donkey for several years. In the customs of Luoyang, on the Cold Food Festival, we take wine and food to offer at the grave. This person took the donkey to the grave, which was east of the Yi River. He wanted to cross the Yi River, but the donkey refused to cross. He whipped its head and face, and it was injured and bleeding. Having arrived at the grave, he released the donkey and offered the sacrifice. Soon, he lost the donkey, and it was still in the same place. On that day, my sister was alone in her brother's house. Suddenly, she saw her mother enter, with blood streaming from her head and face, looking emaciated and worn out. She cried and told her daughter, 'I avoided your brother in life.'"

A woman was sent five sheng of rice. Because of this, she was punished. She took the form of a donkey. She had been repaying her brother for five years. Now she wanted to cross the Yi River, but feared the deep water. Her brother beat her with a whip, breaking her head and face. He still allowed her to return home, but beat her even harder. She came to tell you, saying, "I am about to finish paying off my debt. You can't be too unreasonable in your suffering." After speaking, she walked out. Searching for her, she was not found. The girl remembered the place of his injuries. Afterward, the brother returned. The girl first observed the donkey's head and face injuries. She held him and wept. The brother asked her in surprise. The girl reported the situation. The brother also said he initially refused to cross. And the state when he was lost and found was the same. Thus, the brother and sister, holding each other, wept bitterly. The donkey also shed tears and would not eat water or grass. The brother and sister knelt and pleaded, "If you are our mother, we wish for you to eat grass." The donkey then ate grass, but afterward stopped again. The brother and sister did not know what to do. They then prepared millet and beans and sent them to the place of the five precepts. The donkey then resumed eating and drinking. Later, the donkey died. The sister collected and buried him.

Kang Bao, a scholar-official from the south, was young and had a distinguished scholarly reputation. In the ninth year of the Daji era (613 CE), Yang Xuanwu led a rebellion. Kang Bao's brother received an official position from Yang Xuanwu, and as a result, Kang Bao was implicated and should have been executed but fled secretly, hiding himself in the capital city. By the tenth year of the Daji era (614 CE), Kang Bao took advantage of the opportunity to enter the Secretariat, searching for old acquaintances. At that time, Emperor Yang was not present, and all the gates of the imperial city were closed, except for the Anshang gate, which was the only one open for entry and exit. Just as Kang Bao was entering the gate, he met an old acquaintance named Zeng, who was also from the south and was in charge of the留守 (remaining in charge when the emperor leaves). Zeng saw Kang Bao and spoke with him, asking about his well-being. Kang Bao recognized that they were familiar with each other and told him the truth. After parting and entering, Zeng sent someone to chase and capture him. Kang Bao entered the Secretariat, and the pursuers captured him and reported to the authorities. At that time, Wang Shao was the Deputy Secretary of the Secretariat and had previously been acquainted with Kang Bao. He did not want to punish him and greeted the pursuers, saying, "I knew Kang Bao long ago." Kang Bao understood his intention and responded immediately, saying, "In fact, I am a southern conscript avoiding military service." Wang Shao dismissed him and ordered him to leave. The pursuers returned to report to Zeng.

Zeng again captured him at the Anmen Gate. Bao knew he could not escape. He told Zeng, "I truly owe the government; death is my own due. However, I do not owe you anything. You and I were once close friends, but you could not help me. Why did you turn against me? If the dead have consciousness, I will surely repay you." Bao eventually surrendered to the law. After several days, Zeng's residence was in Taiping Lane, and he was about to enter the residence of the military governor, passing through the Good Harmony Lane and entering the western gate. Suddenly, he saw Bao riding a horse, well-dressed and fresh, with two young servants following behind. Bao said to Zeng, "My life is coming to an end, but I still have three more years to live. Leave my fate in your hands. I now serve as the head of the main office in Tai'an, and I have already petitioned the celestial bureaucracy to punish you." Zeng knelt and apologized, asking for blessings. Bao agreed and then disappeared suddenly. After several more days, Zeng met Bao again. Bao said, "I will eventually kill you. Give yourself seven days to perform good deeds. After that, I will take your head first. If you do not believe me, your face will be on your back when you die." Filled with fear, Zeng returned home. He died as predicted, and his face was indeed found on his back. This story is from the *Kangqin Shi Ren Shuo* (康亲识人说).

During the Wu德 era, a man from Linqiong named Wei made a vow with a woman that he would not burden or harm her. However, after losing her favor, she grew resentful. Fearing that her resentment would affect him, Wei strangled her to death. The name of the woman was Wei Shangxiang (likely a misreading or misinterpretation, as "量其从兄也" suggests Wei Xiahé, his cousin). Wei subsequently fell ill and died of a skin disease, possibly leprosy. In the first month of the sixth year of the Wu德 era, Ma Jiayun, a man from Wei Prefecture, was at home. Late in the evening, he went out through the main gate and suddenly saw two people, each holding a horse, standing under a tree outside the gate. Ma Jiayun asked, "Who are you?" They replied, "We are messengers from the Duke of the East Sea, sent to take you away." Ma Jiayun, who had a good education and often invited official envoys and distinguished guests from all directions to meet him, did not find the summons strange. He told the messengers, "I have no horse." The messengers then presented a horse and said, "Use this to take Ma Jiayun away." Ma Jiayun climbed onto the horse and left, but in reality, he had fallen unconscious under the tree. Soon, he arrived at an official office. As he was about to enter the main gate, he saw several dozen men and women standing outside, like petitioners. Among them was a woman whom Ma Jiayun had previously met. She was the wife of Zhang Gongjin, a man from the same prefecture, and her surname was Cui. Holding a document, she said to Ma Jiayun, "Do you still remember me, Ma Jiayun?" She continued, "We used to associate with Zhang Gongjin, often meeting. Zhang Gongjin acted improperly and killed me unjustly. I have appealed to the heavenly court, and it has been three years now."

For the king, the chief deity, to rescue the cautious and prudent, they are often held back. Now that the opportunity to declare one's position has been seized, the official has already pursued it. It will not be long before it arrives. Suspecting that I alone have been wronged or harmed, will the horse (or person named Ma) also come? Jiayun knows that the Cui family has been killed. Upon seeing, only then does one know of the death. The envoy led him into the gate. The gatekeeper said that the official is sleeping. It is not yet possible to pay respects. It is appropriate to lead him to sit at the place of the Chief Executioner Ho. Jiayun saw the Chief Executioner, who was the Chief Judge Ho Zhang of the Yizhou Commandery. Seeing Jiayun, he invited him to sit and said, "There is a vacancy for the position of secretary in this office. Donghai Gong has heard of your talent and learning. He wishes to appoint you to this position." Jiayun replied, "My family is poor, and my wife and children are not well-established. I hope you will speak on my behalf, and it would be fortunate if I could be exempted." Ho Zhang said, "If so, I can claim that I am unlearned. I will have a way to clarify this." Soon, someone came and said, "The official has already awakened from sleep." They led Jiayun inside. They saw a person sitting in the hall, who was fat, short, and dark-skinned. He called Jiayun forward and said, "Hearing of your talent and learning, I wish to appoint you as secretary. Can you do it?" Jiayun bowed and thanked him, saying, "Very fortunate. But I am a rustic person. I have been involved in teaching the young in the classics."

The text you provided contains some unusual and potentially mistranslated phrases, making it challenging to produce a coherent and accurate translation. However, I will do my best to provide a translation based on the available information. Here is the final translation: --- He was not sufficient to undertake the duties of a secretary or recorder. The official said, "Do you know Ho Zang?" He replied, "I know him." The official then sent for Ho Zang and asked about the talents and skills of Jia Yun. Ho Zang said, "I have always known him for his Confucian learning, but I have not seen him write articles." The official said, "Let the horse go home." He immediately ordered Chen Ziliang to be summoned. Jia Yun excused himself and left. Ho Zang said to him, "Please tell my three dogs at home. Before I die, I want to tell you. Sell my riding horse and build a pagoda. You shouldn't sell the horse for your own expenses. Act quickly as I have taught you. The three dogs who build the pagoda are my eldest sons." Jia Yun then asked, "What did I see Zhang Gongjin's wife say about the 'heavenly lord'?" Ho Zang said, "Wang Wujie, a fellow villager of Zhang Gongjin, died and became the heavenly lord, who often saves Zhang Gongjin. That is why he has survived until now. But it seems he cannot avoid it now." After finishing, they parted. The official sent an envoy to escort Jia Yun. They arrived at a small, narrow path and were instructed to return by this road. Jia Yun entered the path and came back to life. After a long time, he was able to stand up. It was nearly midnight. His wife and children were all sitting and crying. Jia Yun explained everything to them. That year, in July, Chen Ziliang, a man from Mianzhou, died suddenly. --- Please note that some parts of the text, such as "放马生归" and "作乌浮图," are particularly ambiguous and may require further context or verification to ensure accuracy.

After staying overnight, he recovered. He said to himself that he had seen the Lord of the East Sea who wanted to appoint him as a secretary, but he declined because he did not know how to read or write. There was another person from Wu named Chen Ziliang, who was skilled in writing, and so the Lord ordered Chen to take his place. The following year, Chen Ziliang died, and Zhang Gongjin also passed away. After the two men died, Jiayun once traveled with several others, and on the road they suddenly saw officials. Jiayun was filled with fear, and he hurriedly obeyed their commands. After a while, he calmed down. His companions asked him about it, and he replied, "What I saw earlier were messengers from the Lord of the East Sea. They said they were going to Yizhou to pursue someone. They also mentioned that Chen Ziliang had pleaded for me greatly, and that Ho the judicial official had recited something on my behalf. I nearly could not escape, but thanks to my merit in saving lives, I was spared." When Jiayun was in Shu, the people of Shu were about to drain a pond to catch fish. At that time, Jiayun was teaching books to others and received several bolts of silk as payment. He then bought the fish and released them, which was an act of redemption. During the Zhenguan era, when the imperial carriage was at the Jiu Cheng Palace, the emperor heard of this and sent the Secretary of the Central Secretariat, Cen Wenben, to inquire about the matter. Cen recorded it in detail and submitted it to the emperor on Jiayun's behalf. Later, Jiayun became a Doctor of the Imperial Academy and died in office.

In the early years of the Tang Dynasty, Kong Ke, a councilor of the military governor's office in Suizhou, suddenly fell ill and died. After one day, he revived and recounted his experience. He said that he was taken to the official's place, where he was questioned about why he had killed two cows and water. Kong Ke replied that he had not killed them. The official said, "Your brother testified that you killed them. Why did you not confess?" He then called for Kong Ke's brother, who had already been dead for several years. When the brother arrived, he was bound and shackled very severely. The official asked, "Is it true that your brother killed the cows?" The brother said, "My brother was previously on a mission to recruit and pacify the Lao. He ordered me to kill the cows for a gathering. It was indeed following his command, not a suicide." Kong Ke then said, "It is true that I sent my brother to kill the cows for the Lao gathering. It was a matter of state. How can I be guilty?" The official said, "You killed the cows for the Lao gathering, wanting to claim credit for pacification and seek rewards and personal gain. How can you say it was a matter of state?" The official then told Kong Ke's brother, "Because you testified against your brother, I have kept you here. Now that your brother has confessed to sending you, you are not guilty. I will let you be reborn." After saying this, the brother suddenly disappeared. Kong Ke also did not get a chance to speak further. The official then asked Kong Ke, "Why did you also kill two other ducks?" Kong Ke said, "The previous county magistrate killed the ducks to serve official guests. How can it be my fault?" The official said, "Official guests have their own food supplies and do not need ducks. You killed them to serve them, seeking praise. How is it not a crime?" The official then asked, "Why did you also kill six chicken eggs?" Kong Ke said, "I never ate chicken eggs. I only remember when I was nine years old."

On the Cold Food Festival, it was said that the mother cooked six eggs for herself. The official replied affirmatively. The question arose whether to blame the mother. 恪 said, "I dare not. I am only explaining the reason." He said, "It was I who killed them." The official said, "You have taken another's life. You must bear the consequences." After he spoke, suddenly dozens of people in green clothes took 恪 away. 恪 cried out, "The government is also unjustly and excessively harsh." The official heard this and called them back. He asked, "What is unjust?" 恪 said, "All my sins have been recorded, but my good deeds have not been noted. Is this not excessive?" The official asked the supervisor, "What good deeds has 恪 done, and why are they not recorded?" The supervisor replied, "Good deeds are also recorded, but the balance of sins and good deeds is considered. If good deeds outweigh sins, one first receives good fortune; if sins outweigh good deeds, one first receives punishment." The official angrily said, "Even if one first receives punishment, why not announce the good fortune to show it?" He ordered the supervisor to be beaten a hundred times. The beating was swift, and blood splattered on the ground. Soon after, the good deeds 恪 had performed were announced, and they were also not forgotten. The official said to 恪, "You should first receive punishment. I will release you to return home for seven days. You may diligently pursue good deeds." He sent 恪 out, and 恪 recovered. 恪 then gathered monks and nuns to perform rituals and confess. He diligently practiced religious rituals and recounted the story. On the seventh day, he bid farewell to his family. Soon after, he died. His brother, who was a government official, took care of the affairs.

The Duke of Zan, Governor of Lozhou, Dou Gui, was known for his cruel and violent nature. Initially serving as the Shengtai Pushe in Yizhou, he killed many soldiers and also harmed the Shengtai Shangshu Wei Yunqi. During the second year of the Zhenguan era, while in Lozhou, he fell seriously ill. Suddenly, he said, "Someone sent me a melon." Those around him reported, "There are no melons in winter." The duke replied, "A plate of good melons. How can there be none?" He then exclaimed in shock, "It's not melons; they are all heads, owing their lives to me." He then said, "Help me up. I want to see Wei Shangshu." After speaking, he died.

Cui Xingzhi, Secretary of the Ministry of Punishment, was from Cao Ling. By nature, he did not believe in Buddhism and spoke with disdain. He died of illness in the fifth month of the second year of Yonghui. By the ninth day of the sixth month, Wang (surname, name missing), Inspector of the Department of Civil Affairs, suddenly died of illness. After two days, he revived and claimed that at the time of his initial death, he saw four people come to him and say, "Summon you." He followed along and entered a large gate. He saw a hall with a western room, where an official was sitting, fat and black in appearance. In the eastern room of the hall, there was a monk sitting, facing each other, both facing north. Each had a bed, pillow, and table. There were about two hundred attendants, some wearing hats, some not, but all were handsome. There were official documents for officials. There was an old man, wearing a collar, facing west, bound and standing at the bottom of the stairs. When he reached the courtyard, he was also bound. An official held paper and a pen and interrogated him. He said, "In the eighteenth year of Zhenguan, I served as an assistant historian in Chang'an. Why was the register changed?" He replied, "It is not my fault." The high-ranking official in the hall read his words and made a distinction. He turned and said to the old prisoner at the eastern steps, "Why falsely accuse him?" The old prisoner said, "The age of Suddhodana was not yet reached. Because of me, the register was changed, adding to the age of Suddhodana. I did not dare to falsely accuse." He said, "In the seventeenth year, the position was changed. The appointment document was at home. Please investigate it." The high-ranking official then called three people who led him to be untied and went to retrieve the appointment document. The appointment document arrived.

The official read the document himself. He said to the old prisoner, "Your appointment was clearly defined. You have no reason." He then ordered the prisoner to go out through the northern gate. The prisoner saw the northern gate area as dark, with many walls and battlements, suggesting a dangerous place. The official wrote on his desk and said to the prisoner, "You are innocent, go free." The prisoner bowed and took leave. The official led the prisoner to the eastern steps, where the prisoner bowed to a monk, and the monk stamped the prisoner's arm with a seal, saying "Go well." The official led the prisoner out through the eastern gate, passing through three gates, each time having his arm stamped checked before being allowed to proceed. At the fourth gate, it was very large, with red-painted towers, three doors open, resembling an official city gate, with strict guards checking the seal before allowing passage. The prisoner walked southeast for several dozen steps and heard someone call him. The prisoner turned around and saw a court official named Song Xingzhi, who appeared disheveled and dressed in an old red robe, standing at the steps of a hall with guards present. Near the city wall, there was a large wooden sign, about ten feet high and two feet wide, inscribed with the words "This is for the examination and approval of the king's people." The sign was about a foot square and very clear. The hall had a bed, chair, and table, resembling an official office, but no one was present. Song Xingzhi, upon seeing the prisoner, expressed a mix of sadness and joy and asked why the prisoner had come. The prisoner replied, "The official summoned me for investigation, asked about changing my register, and found nothing, so I was released."

A person named Xing Zhi extended both hands. He said to someone, "I have been interrogated about my record of good deeds, but I have no record of good deeds in my hands. Because of this, I am extremely exhausted, and further, I am suffering from hunger and thirst. The suffering is unbearable and cannot be described. You should try hard. Go to my house and speak. Have me perform good deeds. In this manner, I am being diligent and ardent. Thoroughly instruct me." The person then left. After walking dozens of steps, Xing Zhi called the person back. The person had not yet spoken when an official came and sat in the hall, angry and said, "I am just examining various matters. What kind of person are you for going directly to the place of the prisoner?" The official therefore made the person stop and let go of the ear. The person was pushed and ordered to go away. Xing Zhi ran to another door. The doorkeeper said, "You are to let go of the ear. Your ear should be deaf. I will remove the thing inside." The doorkeeper therefore picked the person's ear with their hand, and the person's ear rang. The person was then verified and decided to be released. Going outside the door, it was as black as ink. The person did not know where to go and touched the west and south, finding all were walls. Only the east had no obstacles, but it was dark and the person could not walk. The person stood for a short while and saw the official who had previously questioned them come out from the door. The official said, "Can you still wait for me? That's very good. You can give me a thousand coins." The person did not respond and thought inside themselves, "I have no crime. The official released me to come. Why would there be a corrupt official?" The official immediately said, "You cannot not go. If I had not early taken you to the official, you would have been bound in two days. Wouldn't that be exhausting?" Xing Zhi agreed in his heart and humbly thanked, "I will follow your command." The official said.

I will not use your copper coins. I wish to receive white paper money instead. I promised to meet you on the fifteenth day. [A name or title is missing here] agreed. I then asked about the way home. The official said, "Just walk east for 200 steps. You will see an old wall that is broken through. When you see clearly, you can push it down. Then you will arrive at your home." [Another name or title is missing here] followed his instructions. Walking to the wall, he pushed it. After a long time, it finally fell. [Another name or title is missing here] came out from where it had fallen. He immediately arrived at his residence, the south gate of Longzheng Fang. He then returned home. His family members were crying. Upon entering the house, he revived. By the fifteenth day, [another name or title is missing here] forgot not to give the money. The next day, he became sick again and nearly died. He saw the official come and said angrily, "You really have no trustworthiness. You promised to give me money. Therefore, you did not give it. Now, I will take you away again." He then drove him away. They went out through the Hanguang Gate and ordered him to enter a large pit. [Another name or title is missing here] bowed and thanked him more than a hundred times, asking to make money. He was then let return. He revived again. [Another name or title is missing here] told his family. He bought 100 sheets of paper to make money and sent it. The next day, [another name or title is missing here] became sick again and nearly died. He saw the official again and said, "You fortunately can give me money, but you hate it." [Another name or title is missing here] again apologized and asked to make it again. He agreed. Again, by the twenty-first day, [another name or title is missing here] ordered him to use 60 coins. He bought 100 sheets of white paper to make money and alcohol and food. He burned it in the west channel water of Longzheng Fang. Afterwards, his body became light and healthy and he healed. When he heard the story, he was with the Minister of Punishment Liu Yanke and the Junior Censor of Dali Xing Mao. They were to interrogate the Liu family in the prison. The prime minister asked him about it, and he said so.

During the early years of Emperor Taizong's reign, Wei Waiwang's long-term official, Wei Qingzhi from Jingzhao, had a daughter who had already passed away. The couple mourned deeply. Two years later, Wei Qingzhi planned to gather relatives and guests and prepared meat for a meal. The family bought a sheep but had not yet slaughtered it. That night, Wei Qingzhi's wife had a dream in which she saw her deceased daughter, dressed in a green skirt and white shirt, with her hair adorned with a pair of jade hairpins, which she usually wore. The daughter came to see her mother and wept, saying, "I used things without telling my parents, and due to this karma, I am now in the body of a sheep. I come to repay my parents' debt. Tomorrow, I will be slaughtered. The green sheep with a white head is me. I especially wish for your compassion and mercy to spare my life." The mother woke up in shock. In the morning, she went to look at the sheep and found that its neck and shoulders were all white, with two white spots on its head facing each other, in the shape of jade hairpins. The mother wept and told the family not to kill it, waiting for Wei Qingzhi to arrive so they could release it. Soon after, Wei Qingzhi arrived and urged for the meal. The cook reported that the lady did not permit the killing of the green sheep. Wei Qingzhi became angry and ordered it to be killed. The butcher hung the sheep to slaughter, and several guests had already arrived. They saw a beautiful woman who pleaded with the guests, saying, "I am the daughter of Mr. Wei. Please save my life." The guests were shocked and stopped the butcher. The butcher feared Wei Qingzhi's anger and only heard the sheep bleating, so he killed it. Afterward, the guests sat down, and the food was served. The guests all refused to eat. Wei Qingzhi was surprised and asked them. The guests explained everything. Wei Qingzhi grieved and became ill, never recovering. Many scholars in the capital knew about it. Cui Shangfu, a high-ranking official, provided additional testimony, and Yan Shangfu, another high-ranking official, also said so.

A man from Zheng County, Huazhou, named Zhang Faying. When he was young, poor, and rustic, he did not adhere to social norms and etiquette. In the tenth year of the Zhenguan era, he entered Mount Hua to cut branches and encountered a monk sitting in a rock. Zhang Faying approached him and started a conversation. The sky became dark and gloomy, and they sat for a long time, unable to return home, so they stayed overnight. The monk prepared a meal of pine and cypress leaves for Zhang Faying and said, "I have lived here for a long time and do not want outsiders to know. When you leave, be cautious and do not speak about our meeting." The monk then began to speak, saying, "Ordinary people often have many sins and burdens, and after death, they enter a bad realm. Sincere repentance can eliminate these sins." He instructed Zhang Faying to bathe and purify himself, dress in a monk's robe, and perform a ritual of confession and repentance. The monk then left. In the nineteenth year, Zhang Faying fell ill and died. He was buried in the wilderness, lacking a coffin, and was buried with only firewood. After seven days, he revived, pushed himself out of the grave, and returned home. His family was surprised and shocked. They questioned him and learned that he had come back to life, and they were happy. Zhang Faying recounted his experience, saying that when he first died, two people came to take him away and flew south through the air.

Arriving at the government office, he entered the main gate and then patrolled the alleys. On both sides were the offices of officials, with gates and thresholds facing each other, too numerous to count. When Law Yi arrived at one of these offices, he saw an official who scolded the messenger from afar, saying, "This is Zhang Faying from Huazhou. Originally, he was supposed to arrive within three days. Why did it take seven days?" The messenger replied, "Law Yi's family dog is vicious, and there were spells cast by a sorcerer that hit him severely. He showed his back to us, and it was all bruised and swollen." The official said, "For excessive delay, there is fault. I will give him twenty strokes of the cane." After the punishment, blood flowed and splattered on the ground. The official then said, "Take Law Yi to the clerk. The clerk issued the document and ordered him to be sent to the judge." The judge summoned the chief clerk, who took Law Yi's case file. The case files were very numerous, filling a bed. The chief clerk examined them in front of Law Yi, noting that most of the documents had already been marked with red ink and completed, but there were still a few with incomplete sentences. He recorded these and said, "In the eleventh year of the Zhenguan era, Law Yi's father sent him to cut grass. Law Yi turned around and glared, privately cursing his father, showing disobedience."

A fine of eighty strokes. The record began with one entry. Immediately, a monk from a cave came. The judge rose to greet him. The judge asked the monk what the matter was. The monk said, "Zhang Yi is my disciple. His sins have been forgiven through repentance. In the records of the Heavenly Court, his case has been completed. It is unjust to summon him now, as he should not die." The chief clerk said, "Those who have repented, their cases are also completed. As for Zhang's act of cursing his father, although he has repented, the matter has not been resolved." The monk said, "If it is not this way, we should check the records. There should be benefits." The judge ordered the chief clerk to consult the king about the law. The palace is to the east, with grand halls and buildings, and thousands of guards. The monk also went to the king's presence. The king rose to greet the monk and said, "Should you be coming on duty?" The monk replied, "It is not yet your turn on duty. I have a disciple named Zhang Faye who was summoned here. His past sins have all been resolved, and he should not die." The chief clerk then consulted the king about Zhang's act of cursing his father. The king said, "Zhang's act of cursing his father is after repentance."

A special exemption is not appropriate. However, the teacher is coming. I request a special permission to stay for seven days. The monk, named 法義, said to the monk: "Seven days is not a long time, but if I return, I might not see the teacher again. I request to stay and follow the teacher." The monk replied, "Seven days are actually seven years. You should leave quickly." 法義 persistently asked to follow the monk. The monk, due to some reason, agreed. He then requested the king to write a character on his palm and to seal it. The king sealed it, saying, "You should leave quickly. Return home and cultivate virtue. If you do not see me later, present the seal to the king. The king will certainly release you." 法義 then bid farewell. The monk sent someone to accompany him to his home. The house was completely dark, and 法義 was too afraid to enter. The messenger pushed him in. He then survived, becoming aware that he was in the ground, feeling very light and airy. He pushed and managed to get out. He then entered the mountains, beginning to practice the Way with the mountain monks. The text on the palm where the seal was placed was unknown, but all became sores. It could never be healed. It is still there to this day. The king of Longxi, Bocha, lives nearby and is well-informed about the matter. He told this to someone nearby.

Li Zhi'an, from Hedong, was appointed as the magistrate of Changju County in Xing Prefecture during the early years of the Tang Dynasty. One night, he suddenly died. The next day, he woke up. He said that he was first summoned by officials from the underworld and brought to a grand official building. The messenger presented Li Zhi'an to the king, who said, "There is currently a vacancy for one official, so we have come to ask you to take up this position." Li Zhi'an declined, citing his elderly parents, and also stated that his fortune and virtue should not lead to his death yet. The king had it investigated, and it was indeed true. The king then said, "You have not yet reached the time of death. You can temporarily serve as the recorder." Li Zhi'an agreed and bowed to show gratitude. The officials led him to his office, where there were five judges, including Li Zhi'an, making six in total. The office was a large hall with three rooms for people to sit, each with beds and desks. The work was very busy and overwhelming. There was an empty seat on the western side, without a judge. The officials led Li Zhi'an to the empty seat. A group of officials brought documents, took Li Zhi'an's decisions, placed them on the desk, and then stood back down the steps. Li Zhi'an asked them, and they replied, "The air is bad and cannot be changed, so we must not disturb you. Just answer the matters on the desk remotely." Li Zhi'an reviewed the cases as if they were cases in the human world and then immediately made judgments. After a while, food came, and all the judges ate together. Li Zhi'an also joined them. The other judges said, "Since you are temporarily serving as a judge, it is not appropriate for you to eat this."

Zhi Gan followed the instructions. He was finally too afraid to eat. At dusk, the official sent Zhi Gan back home. He woke up and it was already late. Afterward, in the house, it was called dusk. The official would always come to greet him. When he arrived, it was dawn. Therefore, it was known that the realm of spirits and the mortal world were reversed in terms of day and night. Thus, he judged matters of the underworld at night and attended to his county duties during the day. He continued this way as a regular routine. After a year, Zhi Gan was in the underworld office. He got up to go to the toilet and found himself in the west part of the hall, where he saw a woman about thirty years old, with a dignified appearance, wearing bright and clean clothes, standing there and wiping her tears. Zhi Gan asked, "Who are you?" She replied, "I am the wife of the warehouse manager and military officer of Xingzhou. I was brought here by force. I am about to part from my husband. That is why I am sad." Zhi Gan asked the official, who said, "The official brought me here to investigate something. To provide evidence about my husband's affairs." Zhi Gan then advised the woman, "I am Zhi Gan, the magistrate of Changju. If you are questioned, please explain yourself. Do not involve your husband; both of us dying would be pointless." The woman said, "I truly do not want to involve him, but I am afraid the official will involve him." Zhi Gan said, "Please do not involve him. You can avoid the worry of being pressured." The woman agreed. Afterward, Zhi Gan returned to the state and first asked about the condition of the warehouse manager's wife. The warehouse manager said, "My wife is young."

No harm came to him. He told him what he had seen. He described the clothes and appearance of the person he had encountered, and advised him to cultivate merit. The warehouse keeper went to the woman's house and saw her weaving in her loom without harm. He did not believe it at all. After more than ten days, the warehouse keeper's wife suddenly fell ill and died. The warehouse keeper began to fear and started to cultivate merit. There were also two officials from the state of Xingzhou who had enough pearls to be selected for the capital. They asked Zhi Gan, "You judge matters of the underworld. Please ask, what office will we be selected for?" Zhi Gan went to the underworld's office and asked the clerk by their names. The clerk said that the name book was sealed in a stone box and would take two days to check. When he left, the report came back, and he saw two clerks. The names of the offices they were selected for were reported to the two officials. The two officials went to the capital to be selected, and the Ministry of Personnel assigned them to their offices. All were different from what was reported. The state official heard about it and told Zhi Gan. Zhi Gan asked the clerk again, and the clerk checked the records and said, "It is definitely as previously checked. There is no mistake." Afterwards, the two selected officials passed through the Gate of the Lower, which reviewed and returned it to the Ministry of Personnel for reassignment. It turned out to be exactly as reported by the underworld records. Thus, everyone believed and was convinced. Zhi Gan often in the underworld records, saw the names and conditions of his relatives and acquaintances, and the time, month, and day.

Zhi Gan reported the matter. He was instructed to cultivate merit, and many were saved as a result. Zhi Gan was granted a three-year term in office. His subordinate came to inform him that Li Si Hu of Longzhou had been appointed to a formal position to replace him. From then on, Zhi Gan would no longer preside. The next morning, Zhi Gan arrived at the prefecture and reported to the prefect Li Defeng. Li Defeng sent someone to Longzhou to investigate. Upon arrival, it was discovered that the local official had already died. When asked about the matter, it was revealed that this occurred at the time when the official came to report. From then on, the matter ceased. Later, the prefectural office sent Zhi Gan to escort the prisoners to the capital. They reached the border of Fengzhou, but the prisoners all escaped. Zhi Gan was worried and fearful, and after chasing and pursuing for several days, he could not catch them. That night, he stayed at a monastery. Suddenly, he saw his former subordinate ghost official come to report, saying, "The prisoners are all captured. One has already died, and three are in a valley in the south mountains. They have all been captured and bound. Please do not worry." After speaking, the ghost official left. Zhi Gan immediately requested troops and entered the western valley of the south mountains. Indeed, he saw four prisoners. The prisoners knew that running away was futile, so they came to resist. Zhi Gan overpowered them, killing one prisoner and binding the other three. Just as he had been told, everything unfolded as expected. Zhi Gan is still alive today, serving as the Military Governor of Cizhou. The Imperial Steward Liu Hen told me about it. Heng served as the Prefect of Caozhou and asked Zhi Gan personally, saying, "Yes, the Censor Wei Tongjie also said he had heard it. All said it was like this."

冥报记 (唐 唐临) 吏部尚书唐临撰 冥报记卷上 夫含气有生。无不有识。有识而有行。随行善恶而受其报。如农夫之播植。随所植而收之。此盖物之常理。固无所可疑也。上智达其本源。知而无见。下愚闇其踪迹。迷而不返。皆绝言也。中品之人。未能自达。随缘动见。逐见生疑。疑见多端。各怀异执释典论其分别。凡有六十二见。邪倒于是乎生者也。临在中人之后。幸而寤其万一。比见众人不信因果者说见虽多。同谓善恶无报。无报之说。略有三种。一者自然。故无因果。唯当任欲待事而已。二者灭尽。言死而身灭。识无所住。身识都尽。谁受苦乐。以无受故。知无因果。三者无报。

言见今人有修道德。贫贱则早死。或行凶恶。富贵灵长。以是事故。知无因果。临窃谓。儒书。论善恶之报甚多。近者报于当时。中者报于于累年之外。远者。报于子孙之后。当时报者。若楚子吞蛭。痼疾皆愈。宋公不祷。妖星多退。黍齿凶逆。旋踵伏诛。赵高惑乱。俄而灭族之类。是也。累年报者。如魏颗嫁妾。终以济师。孙叔埋蛇。竟享多福。汉幽鸩如意。苍茍成灾。齐杀彭生。立豕而崇之类是也。子孙报者。若弗父恭于三命。广宣尼之道。邓训岁活千人。遗和熹之庆。陈平阴计。自知无后。栾黡黍侈。盈被其殃之类。是也。若乃虞舜以孝行登位。周文以仁贤受命。

桀纣以残忍亡国。幽厉以淫纵祸终。三代功德。下祚长久。秦皇骄暴。及子而灭。若斯之比。触类寔繁。虽复大小有殊。亦皆善恶之验。但事法王道。理关天命。常谈之际。非所宜言。今之所录。盖直取其微细验。冀以发起同类。贻告子孙。徵于人鬼之间。若斯而已也。释氏说教。无非因果。因即是作果。即是报。无一法而非因。无一因而不报。然其说报亦有三种。一者现报。于此身中。作善恶业。即于此身而受报者。皆名现报。二者生报。谓此身作业。不即受之。随业善恶。生于诸道。皆名生报。三者后报。谓过去身作善恶业。能得果报。应多身受。

是以现在作业。未便受报。或次后后生受。或五生十生。方始受之。是皆名后报。于此三报。摄一切法。无所不尽。是今诸见复然大寤。然今俗士尚有惑之。多习因而忘果。疑耳而信目。是以闻说后报。则若存若亡。见有效验。则惊嗟信服。昔晋高士谢敷。宋尚书令傅高。太子中书舍人报演。齐司徒事中郎陆果。或一时令望。或当代名家。并录观世音应验记。及齐竟陵王萧子良作宣验记。王琰作冥祥记。皆所以徵明善恶。劝戒将来。实使闻者深心感寤。临既慕其风旨。亦思以劝人。辄录所闻。集为此记。仍具陈所受。及闻见由缘。言不饰文。事专扬确。庶后人见者。能留意焉。

随京师大德沙门。释信行。本相州法藏寺僧。初其母无子。久以为忧。有沙门过之。劝念观世音菩萨。母曰夜祈念。顷之有娠。生信行。幼而聪慧。博学经论。识达过人。以为佛所说经。务于济度。或随根性。指人示道。或逐时宜。因事判法。今去圣久远。根时久异。若以下人修行上法。法不当根。容能错倒。乃钞集经论。参验人法所当学者。为三十六卷。名曰人集录。开皇初。左仆射齐公。闻其盛名。奏文帝。徵诣京师。住公所造真寂寺。信行又据经律。录出三阶法四卷。其大旨。劝人普敬。认恶本。观佛性。当病授药。顿教一乘。自天下。勇猛精进之士。皆宗之。信行尝头陀乞食。六时礼拜。劳力定心。空形实智而已。每坐禅说法。常见青衣童子四人。持花立侍。尝与徒众。在堂中坐禅。众人忽闻奇香。光照堂内。相共怪异。咨问信行。

信行令问弟子僧邕惠如。邕曰。向见化佛从空中来。至禅师前。摩顶授记。如云亦摩邕顶授记。余状与邕说同。后邕典其徒众。隐太白山。一旦请众僧曰。当与师等还京。众敬邕皆从之。即下山。夜宿武功。未明便发。谓众曰。师等努力。今暝必须入城。曰没至漕上。闻鼓音叹曰。城门闭矣。遂宿于逆旅。至昏时。悲泣曰。无所及矣。众问其故不答。明早入城。至真寂寺。而信行昨夜昏时气绝。寺僧怪问邕来。答曰。在山遥见多人持香花幡盖。从西来入开远门。向真寂寺。邕疑禅师欲去。故来也。昨夜昏时。见禅师导从西去。顾与邕别。故知不及也。初京城诸师。有疑信行法者。至是相与议。据付法藏经。若人通身。过去闻正法故。于是。共观信行头骨。两耳正通。乃皆惭悔信服。初信行。徒众居京城五寺。后虽侵广。今犹号五禅师（老僧。及临舅说云尔）。

京城真寂寺沙门慧如。少精勤苦行。师事信行。信行亡后。奉遵其法。隋大业中。因坐禅修定。遂七曰不动。众皆叹异之。以为入三昧也。既而慧如开目。涕泣交流。僧众怪问之。答曰。火烧脚痛。待视疮毕乃说。众逾怪问。慧如曰。被阎罗王请。行道七曰满。王问。须见先亡知识不。如答曰。欲见二人。王即遣唤一人。唯见龟来。舐慧如足。目中泪出而去。更一人者。云罪重不可唤。令就见之。使者引慧如至狱门。门闭甚固。使者唤守者。有人应声。使者语慧如。师急避道。莫当门立。如始避而门开。大火从门流出。如锻铁者一星。迸着如脚。如以被拂之。举目视门。门已闭讫。竟不得相见。王施绢三十匹。固辞不许云。已遣送后房。众僧争往。后房视之则绢在床矣。其脚烧疮。大如钱。百余曰乃愈。武德初卒。真寂寺。即今化度寺是也（此寺。临外祖齐公所立。常所游观。每闻舅氏说。云尔）。
绛州大德沙门。释僧彻。少而精练。于孤山西阿。造立堂宇。多树林等。颇得山居形胜。僧彻尝出行山间。土穴中。见一癞病人。疮痍臭秽。从彻乞食。彻愍之。呼出与归。于精舍旁。为造土穴。给衣食。教令诵法华经。此人不识文字。性又顽鄙。彻句句授之。殊费功力。然终不懈倦。此人诵经向半。便梦。有人教之。自后稍聪寤。至得五六卷。渐觉疮愈。比诵一部毕。须眉平复。生肥体如常。而能为疗疾。临尝患肿。僧彻遣此人。禁咒有验。

自说云然。后房仁裕为秦州刺史。表僧彻所立精舍。为陷泉寺。初此地无水。僧彻常远汲山下。以自供。一朝忽有陷。陷处泉出。故因以名陷寺也。僧专以劝善为务。而自修禅业。远近崇敬如父焉。永徽二年正月。忽属累徒众。自言将死。既而端坐绳床。闭目不动。其天气晴朗。而花如雪。香而不消。方二里许。树叶上皆有白色。如轻粉者三曰。乃复常色。而僧彻已终。至今三岁。独坐如故。亦不臭怀。唯目泪下云（彻弟子实秦等。及州人并说云尔也）。

河东有练行尼。常诵法华经。访工书者一人。数倍酬直。特为净室。令写此经。一起一浴。燃香熏衣。仍于写经之室。凿壁通外。加一竹筒。令写经人。每欲出息。辄遣含竹筒。吐气壁外。写经一卷。八年乃毕。供养严重。尽其恭敬。龙门僧法端。常集大众。讲法华经。以此尼经本精定。遣人请之。尼固辞不与。法端责让之。尼不得已。乃自送付。法端等。开读。唯见黄纸。了无文字。更开余卷。皆悉如此。法端等惭惧。即送还尼。尼悲泣受。以香水洗函。沐浴顶戴。绕佛行道。于七曰七夜。不暂休息。既而开视。文字如故。贞观二年。法端自向临说（当具说尼名字。临忘之。唯记其事云尔）。

蒲州仁寿寺僧。释道县。少聪慧。好学。为州里所崇敬。讲涅槃八十余遍。号为精熟。贞观二年。崔义直任虞卿县令。人请县讲经。初发题。悲泣。谓众人曰。去圣遥远。微言隐绝。庸愚所传。不足师范。但以信心归向。自当识悟。今之讲说。止于师子。时曰既没。愿各在心。既而讲至师子。一旦无疾而卒。道俗惊恸。义直身自徒跣。送之南山之阴。时十一月。土地冰冻。下尸于地。地即生花。如莲而小。头及手足。各有一花。义直奇之。令人夜守。守者疲睡。有人盗折其花。明旦视之。周身并有花出。总五百余茎。经七曰乃萎干（义直及道俗皆说云尔）。

河东沙门。释道英。少修禅行。以练心为本。不慎威仪。然而经律奥义。莫不一闻县解。远近僧尼。争就请决。英辄报谓曰。汝尚未疑。宜且思疑。凝成然后来问。问者还而思疑多。固思自解而去。有思而不寤。重来问者英为说其机要。皆喜寤而还。尝与众人。乘船黄河。中流船没。众人皆死。道俗望见英没。临河恸哭。是时冬末。河冰始泮。两岸犹坚。英乃水中出行至岸。穿冰而去。岸人敬喜。争欲解衣衣之。英曰。体中尚热。勿覆衣也。徐出而归。了无寒色。视其身体。如火炙处。其识者以为入定故也。或时为人牧牛驾车。食蒜啖饭。或着俗衣。发长数寸。尝至仁寿寺。道悬敬安处之。曰晚求食。悬谓曰。上德虽无食相。岂不为息讥嫌。英笑答曰。悬公心方驰惊。不暂休一息。而空饥饿。何自苦也。道悬叹服。贞观中卒（法端。及道俗皆说云尔）。

幽州沙门释智苑。精练有学识。隋大业中。发心造石经藏之。以备法灭。既而于幽州北山。凿岩为石室。即磨四壁。而以写经。又取方石。别更磨写。藏诸室内。每一室满。即以石塞门。用铁锢之。时随赐帝幸涿郡。内史侍郎萧瑀皇后之同母弟也。性笃信佛法。以其事白后。后施绢千匹。余钱物。以助成之。瑀亦施绢五百匹。朝野闻之。争共舍施。故苑得遂其功。苑尝以役匠既多。道俗奔凑。欲于岩前。造木佛堂。并食堂寝屋。而念木瓦难辨。恐分费经物。

故未能起作。一夜暴雨雷电震山。明旦既晴。乃见山下有大松柏数千株。为水所漂流。积道次。山东少林木。松柏尤稀。道俗惊骇。不知来处。推寻踪迹。远自西山。崩岸倒木。漂送来此。于是远近叹服。谓为神助。苑乃使匠择取其木。余皆分与邑里。邑里喜悦。而共助造堂宇。顷之毕成。皆如其志焉。苑所造石满七室。以贞观十三年卒。弟子犹继其功（殿中丞相里玄奘。大理丞釆宣明等。皆为临说云尔。临以十九年从车驾幽州。问乡人。亦同云尔而以军事不得云）。

东魏末。邺下人。共入西山采银钞。出穴未毕。而穴崩。有一人在后。为石塞门不得出。而无伤损。其穴崩处。有小穴不合。微见曰光。此人自念终无理。乃一心念佛。其父闻子已压。无处求尸。家又贫窭。无以追福。乃持粗饭一钵。住诣僧寺。请一人斋。众僧多逐丰厚。莫肯为食。父持饭大哭。有一僧愍之受请。食讫为咒愿。因别去。是曰中其子在穴中。忽于小穴明处。见一沙门。从穴中入来。持一钵饭。以授此人。食讫。便不复饥。唯端坐正念。经十余年。齐文皇帝即位。于西山造凉殿。匠工除此崩石。乃见穴中人尚活。出之与归。父母惊喜。遂阖练行（雍州司马卢承业。为临说云。是著作郎降所傅之）。

北齐时。有冀州人。徒军伐梁。战败。见擒为奴。其父母在乡不知音问。谓以死。为追福。造塼浮图。塼浮图成。设斋会。道俗数百人。方坐食。闻叩门声。主人父出视。见一僧。形容甚雅。谓主人曰。乞斋食黍糜。可以布手巾裹之。并乞鞋一量。主人请留住食。僧不肯曰。欲早去。不暇坐食也。主人如其言。以新布裹糜。并鞋一量奉之。僧受而去。是曰斋时。主人子在江南泽中。为其主牧牛。忽见一僧手持糜一裹。及新鞋一量。至奴所。问曰。亦思归见父母乎。奴泣曰。无敢望也。僧以与糜令坐食。食毕。又与鞋令着之。而敷袈裟于地。令坐袈裟上。僧取袈裟四角。总把擎举而挥之。可移二丈许着地。奴开视。不见僧及袈裟。而身已在其宅门外。入门见。大众方食。父母惊喜。就问。具说由缘。视其巾内。余糜及鞋。乃向奉僧者也。乡邑惊骇。竞为笃信。是曰。月六曰也。因名所造浮图。为六曰浮图。浮图今尚。邑里犹传之矣。

北齐时。有冀州人。徒军伐梁。战败。见擒为奴。其父母在乡不知音问。谓以死。为追福。造塼浮图。塼浮图成。设斋会。道俗数百人。方坐食。闻叩门声。主人父出视。见一僧。形容甚雅。谓主人曰。乞斋食黍糜。可以布手巾裹之。并乞鞋一量。主人请留住食。僧不肯曰。欲早去。不暇坐食也。主人如其言。以新布裹糜。并鞋一量奉之。僧受而去。是曰斋时。主人子在江南泽中。为其主牧牛。忽见一僧手持糜一裹。及新鞋一量。至奴所。问曰。亦思归见父母乎。奴泣曰。无敢望也。僧以与糜令坐食。食毕。又与鞋令着之。而敷袈裟于地。令坐袈裟上。僧取袈裟四角。总把擎举而挥之。可移二丈许着地。奴开视。不见僧及袈裟。而身已在其宅门外。入门见。大众方食。父母惊喜。就问。具说由缘。视其巾内。余糜及鞋。乃向奉僧者也。乡邑惊骇。竞为笃信。是曰。月六曰也。因名所造浮图。为六曰浮图。浮图今尚。邑里犹传之矣。

梁武帝微时。识一寒士。及即位。游于苑中。见牵舟。帝问之。尚贫贱如故。敕曰。明曰可上谒。吾当与汝县令。此人奉敕而往。会故不得见。频往遇有事。终不得通。自怪之以问沙门宝志。志方为大众讲经。听者数千人。寒士不得进。宝志谓。众曰。有人欲来见问。请开道内之。众人乃为开。此人进未至。宝志迎谓曰。君为不得县令来问耶。终不得矣。但受虚恩耳。过去。帝为斋主。君其疏许施钱五百。而竟不与。是故今曰但蒙许官。终不得也。此人闻之终去。帝亦更不求之（江东道俗至今传之）。

杨州严恭者。本泉州人。家富于财。而无兄弟。父母爱恭。言无所违。陈太建初恭年弱冠。请于父母。愿得钱五万。往杨州市物。父母从之。恭乘船载钱。而下去杨州。数十里。江中逢一船载鼋。将诣市卖之。恭问知其故。念鼋当死。请赎之。鼋主曰。我鼋大头。千钱乃可。恭问有几头。答有五十。恭曰。我正有钱五万。愿以赎之。鼋主喜取钱。付鼋而去。恭尽以鼋放江中。空船诣杨州。其鼋主。别恭行十余里。船没而死。是曰恭父母在家。昏时有乌衣客五十人。诣门寄宿。并送钱五万。付恭父曰。君儿在杨州市。附此钱归。愿依数受也。恭父怪愕疑谓恭死。因审之。客曰。儿无恙。但不须钱。故附归耳。恭父受之。记是本钱。而皆水湿。留客为设食。客止。明旦辞去。后月余曰。恭还。父母大喜。既而问附钱所由。恭答无之。父母说客形状。及附钱月曰。乃赎鼋之曰。于是知五十客。皆所赎鼋也。父子惊叹。

因共往杨州起精舍。专写法华经。遂徙家杨州。家转富。大起房廊为写经之室。庄严清净。供给丰厚。书生常数十人。杨州道俗。共相崇敬。号曰严法华。尝有知亲。从从贷经钱一万。恭不获已与之。贷者受钱。以船载归。中路船倾。所贷之钱落水。而船人不溺。是曰恭入钱库。见有万湿钱如斯出水。恭甚怪之。后见前贷钱人。乃知湿钱是所贷者。又有商人。至宫湖。于神所。祭酒食。并上物。其夜梦。神送物还之。谓曰。倩君为我持此。奉严法华。以供经用也。且而所上神物。皆在其前。于是商人叹异。送达恭处。而倍加厚施。其后。恭至市买经纸。适遇少钱。忽见一人。持钱三千。授恭曰。君买纸。言毕不见。而钱在其前。怪异如此。非一。隋开皇末恭死。子孙传其业。隋季盗贼至江都者。皆相与约勿入严法华里。里人赖之获全。其家今。写经不已（州邑共见。京师人士亦多知之。驸马守国公萧锐最所详审也）。

冥报记卷中 隋开皇中。魏州刺史博陵崔彦武。因行部。至一邑。愕然惊喜。谓从者。吾昔尝在此邑中。为人妇。今知家处。回马入修巷。曲至一家叩门。命主人。主人公年老。走出拜谒。彦武入家。先升其堂。视东壁上。去地六七尺有高隆。谓主人曰。吾昔所读法华经。并金钗五只。藏此壁中。高处是也。经第七卷尾后纸。火烧失文字。吾至今。每诵此经。至第七卷尾。恒忘失不能记。因令左右凿壁。果得经函。开第七卷尾。及金钗。并如其言。主人涕泣曰。亡妻存曰。常读此经。钗亦是亡妻之物。妻因产死。遂失所在。不淫使君。乃示其处。彦武亦云。庭前槐树。吾欲产时。自解头发。置此树空中。试令人探。果得发。主人于是主人悲喜。彦武留衣物。厚给主人而去。崔尚书敦礼说云然。往年见卢文励。亦同。但言齐州刺史不得姓名。不崔具。仍依崔录。

隋大业中。有客僧。行至大山庙。求寄宿。庙令曰。此无别舍。唯神庙庑下。可宿。然而比来。寄宿者辄死僧曰。无苦也。令不得已从之为设床于庑下。僧至夜端坐。诵经可一更。闻屋中环佩声。须臾神出。为僧礼拜。僧曰。闻比来宿者多死岂檀越害之耶。愿见护。神曰。遇其死时将至。闻弟子声。因自惧死。非杀之也。愿师无虑。僧因延坐。谈说如人。良久。僧问曰。闻世人传说。大山治鬼。宁有之也。神曰。弟子薄福有之。岂欲见先亡已乎。僧曰。有两同学僧。先死。愿见之。神问名曰。一人已生人间。一在人狱。罪重不可见。与师就见可也。僧甚悦。因共起出门。不遂而至一所。多见庙狱。

火光甚盛。神将僧入一院。遥见一人在火中号呼不能言。形变不可复识。而血肉焦臭。令人伤心。此是也。师不复欲历观也。僧愁愍求出。俄而至庙。又与神同坐。因问欲救同学。有得理耶。神曰。可得耳。能为写法华经者。便免。既而将曙。神辞僧入堂。旦而庙令视僧不死。怪异之。僧因为说。仍即为写法华经一部。经既成。庄严毕。又将经就庙宿。其夜神出如初。欢喜礼拜。慰问来意。僧以事告。神曰。弟子知之。师为写经。始尽题目。彼已脱免。今久出生不在也。然此处不净洁。不可安经。愿师还送经向寺。言说久之将晓。辞而去。入僧送经于寺（杭州别驾张德言。前任兖州。具知其事。自向临说云尔也）。

国子祭酒萧璟。兰陵人。梁武帝之孙。梁王岿之第五子也。梁灭入隋。姊为炀帝皇后。生长贵盛。而家崇佛法。大业中。自以诵法华经。乃依经文作多宝塔。以檀香为之。塔高三尺许。其上方厚等为木多宝像。经数年。其兄子诠在宅。朝起。忽于前院草中。见一檀木浮图。盖下有一□石佛像。制作异于中国。面形似胡。其眼睛以银为之。中黑精光净如自然者。诠走告璟。璟视惊喜。取盖还。试置塔上。宛然相称。如故作者。虽木色小异。而塔形更妙。以佛像安塔中。亦相称如故造。璟喜欢。自以精诚所感。其佛像函内有舍利百余枚。璟女尼年少。窃疑。胡僧每云。舍利锤打不破。乃试取三十枚。于石上斧打之。

舍利了无著者。女就地拾觅。唯得三四枚。余并失。不见所在。既而惧以告璟。璟往塔中视之。则舍利皆在如旧。璟从此曰诵法华经一遍。以至干身终。贞观十一年病。萧后及弟侄视之。璟与相见。各令燃香因即共别。唯留弟宗公瑀及女为尼者。令燃香诵经。顷之谓其尼曰。我欲去。普贤菩萨来迎。我在东院。师可迎之。尼如言往迎之未还。璟曰。此院不净。不肯来。吾当往就。汝等好住。因与瑀等别。仍起长跪合掌。正向西方。顷之。倒卧遂绝。遗令。独载一车。敛以在身之服。妇人不得送葬。勿以肉祭。制坎才令没棺。朝野叹其通寤。家人奉而行之（仲圭弟孝谐。为大理主簿。为临说。更闻州人亦同云尔）。
